Hey @mrtati ,

thanks for your suggestions and glad you find URH useful!

I see some problems with the changes you made:
* Changing the default modulation type to ASK breaks some unit tests and furthermore in our experience FSK is more common than ASK
* A PNG export in the context menu adds complexity to the menu which is quite full already. Also, your export routine also exports the scroll bars. Furthermore, I could make a screenshot of the signal part I am interested in, so I do not see great value in an PNG export routine.
* Displaying the number of bits in Interpretation is tricky. In your implementation it tells me, I selected 32 bits when selecting the string  " [Pause:". 
![bildschirmfoto_2018-07-23_11-20-35](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18219846/43069326-14b2ab3e-8e6d-11e8-868e-a1415b3da44e.png) 
The __Analysis tab__ is a much better place to make such comparisions.

So all in all I think your changes may help in your specific use case, but should not me merged to upstream.

@andynoack ,
I've added FEC encoding support to the plugin and changed the name of the repository to
[urh-cc1101_FEC_encode_decode_plugin](https://github.com/SpaceTeddy/urh-cc1101_FEC_encode_decode_plugin)
The new version is now able to encode a non FEC encoded payload.

start the plugin in urh as external program with: 
urh_fec_decode_cc1101 [e | d | f]

Parameter description: 
e = encodes a cc1101 FEC unencoded payload 
d = output decoded payload incl. preamble and sync word. note: preamble and sync word are not scrabled 
f = output decoded payload, but preamble and sync word are cropped from the data stream

搜了一圈，最后结论是这个问题在Python 2.x下没有完美解决方案。。。原因是Python 2.x的os.walk()无法完美支持unicode。。。（Python 3的已经早就通过pep383修正，但是。。。这个修正不能back port到Python 2.x，参见这里：http://bugs.python.org/issue22862 ）

既然程序解决不了，只能用改名大法了。

关于技术上的细节，可参见一下讨论：
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/21772271/unicodedecodeerror-when-performing-os-walk
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/16918063/unicodedecodeerror-during-encode
http://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0383/
http://bugs.python.org/issue22862
https://wiki.python.org/moin/UnicodeDecodeError
Thanks :smile:

I added the `--build-wheels` option to `pip2tgz` and `pip2pi` in my fork https://github.com/wolever/pip2pi/compare/master...colinhoglund:master.

When this option is enabled it simply runs `pip wheel` on the passed in packages. This is not terribly efficient since the command `pip2pi /tmp/packages some_package -w -z` would make three different calls to pip. However, any alternative would likely break existing functionality.

Also, adding this functionality makes the entry_point `pip2tgz` somewhat confusing (since it can also build wheels), but changing that also has compatibility implications.

I'd appreciate any feedback/recommendations. Also, let me know if this looks useful and I can submit a PR.

Folks, looking back - it is a very unfortunate decision by developers.
First, `pep8` and `pycodestyle` report all the errors.
Second, `autopep8` is not expected to produce ideal solution for all cases. Having `git` showing all the changes one may simply run `autopep8` to fix all code, making it immediately compliant and equivalent to the source, then come back and fix whatever is done in sub-optimal way.
Definitely long comments could be broken in just any place to make code compliant and equivalent.
A little more intelligence - find a space closest to the boundary and if it is within, say, 10-15 characters from the border, break right there.
We often inherit legacy codebases where nobody cares much about most of old comments, so having `autopep8` being really AUTO would definitely make a difference - with negligible risks.

According to [the pycodestyle docs](http://pycodestyle.pycqa.org/en/latest/intro.html), the relevant check is `E721`, and it can be disabled at the line level using `# noqa`. This is good enough for me.

Again, not too surprising, this is what I saw with some profiles on the web site. DeviantArt allows its users to create any folder structure and then to add any deviation-objects at will to each of them. Now using gallery-dl to download all folders there then naturally results in duplicates.
But I think we should definitely err on the side of duplicates instead of missing some items.
Because 1. most DeviantArt profiles aren't actually that big at all and 2. deduplication can be done on the user's side, lots of programs to choose from here should someone feel this to be necessary.

But okay, bundling this all together in a neat and tidy package seems like the more elegant solution.

A small remark to (13) and (14): Nothing wrong with that suggestion, but alternatively a separate category directory could be used here instead, directly below the top level, i.e. `deviantart\selection` or something, because I think that if someone (again, our hypothetical user) makes the extra step to invoke gallery-dl on an URL to one specific element, it will be done on purpose, I'd assume.

Hello @stickjitb! Unfortunately, NLPIR (the library we use to segment text), uses `/` as the separator between tokens that it segments. Here is a typical example that NLPIR returns for `hi there`
```
hi/o there/rzs
```

You'll notice that it uses spaces between tokens. And, it uses a `/` to separate the token from the part of speech.

In your example, this is returned:
```
[ / ]/xm
```

That breaks the format that the NLPIR project has decided to use for their token separation.

There really isn't anything we can do on the PyNLPIR side about this. You might try talking to the NLPIR team on their GitHub project or website:
https://github.com/NLPIR-team/NLPIR
http://ictclas.nlpir.org/

What this issue showed me is that a lot of people try to solve stuff with try/except in Python instead of fixing the actual issue, which makes me a bit sad.

> The SequenceMatcher is really fast. Do you really think that this is an issue?

Yes. Because [DiffLib is sometimes wrong](http://carreau.github.io/posts/08-Dear-DiffLib.html), using it is shooting yourself in the foot in some rare case. I wrote the above after scratching my head for a week on an incomprehensible problem. 

As to "fast", I've benchmarks somewhere that show that in same case it gets `O(n**2)`  if not worse. 
And that sometime it's "fast" because it does not give you minimal diff, but some crazy results, that are correct diff but **far** from being the expected ones. 

This is a "won't fix" upstream as SequenceMatcher() is made for "human friendly" diff, and they won't change the behavior. The O(n**2) behavior is also what makes it by default set `autojunk=True` which drop the most common character before performing the diff if `len(sequence) > 200`:

```
    # from starting any matching block at a junk element ...
    # b2j also does not contain entries for "popular" elements, meaning
    # elements that account for more than 1 + 1% of the total elements, and
    # when the sequence is reasonably large (>= 200 elements); this can
    # be viewed as an adaptive notion of semi-junk, and yields an enormous
    # speedup when, e.g., comparing program files with hundreds of
    # instances of "return NULL;"
```

So yeah, i've seen a lot of surprising behavior of Sequence Matcher, and have started to write my own a couple of years ago (I should revive that). But I don't trust it to even be fast on2 similars inputs.

> Sorry if I am misinterpreting your implementation, but this seems more like a bug to me than a feature.

Hey, thanks for taking the time to comment on the library, I appreciate it.

**However, this is a _feature_ not a _bug_.**

I've addressed this a few times in the comments section of a few blog posts so it's probably worth including a line about it in the source code but for the vast majority of implementations of real-world computer vision pipelines you will not be processing the same frame multiple times. For highly simplistic operations, yes, totally possible -- but that's an outlier situation. Adding additional code to check for a brand new frame each and every time would further slow down the more "real-world" implementations of problems.

Here I'm defining "real-world" as more computationally heavy, of course. Its totally possible that highly simplistic operations can solve real-world problems in some situations, but if your pipeline is running that fast you would be better off use a blocking `cv2.VideoCapture` to ensure you grab a brand new frame, but again, that's an outlier situation and not why this class was designed.

> The victim is a webserver running an ACME client. It wants to obtain a
certificate. First it creates a private key: openssl genpkey …. Next it needs
a CSR. It uses asn1crypto to load the private key, derive the public key and
assemble the CertificationRequestInfo. Asn1crypto is used here because one
can specify the exact contents of the cri, a flexibility that's not available
from other libs. The result is dumped to DER and signed using a secure ec
implementation. Asn1crypto again is used to combine the signature and the cri
into the csr. The ACME service issues the cert. And the attacker knows the
private key.

I don't understand what asn1crypto is doing in this scenario that is leaking the private key.

> The public_key property can be dangerous to use. The DSA implementation uses python's pow which is probably insecure too. Only the RSA part looks secure because nothing needs to be computed.
A few other functions use public_key, so those would be insecure as well.

If asn1crypto was doing general purpose cryptography, I would definitely consider it to be insecure. My understanding of timing attacks is that they function on measuring the timing of branches to determine information about the key being used. In the case of determining the public key from the private key, this is a single computation that only ever operates on a single value. Executing the same `pow()` function repeatedly won't follow different branches, so no meaningful information is revealed. If the `pow()` function was used repeatedly to sign various values, then the attacker can craft the values to be signed to reveal information about the private key. The elliptic curve computation is similar in that the exact same value is computed each time, thus not revealing any information.

If I am misunderstanding this in some way, and the existence of being able to computer the public key from the private key is a security issue, I'm happy to remove it and instead document ways that users can safely compute the value.

This seems normal to me. When a leader goes down you should expect failed sends unless you are configuring `retries` . Note that not *all* sends will fail, but any that were in-flight will probably fail. You should also expect some delay caused by the metadata refresh cycle whenever a node goes down. `KafkaTimeoutError` is raised specifically when the result of your future is not ready yet. So in this case you want a response in 500 ms and it isn't ready yet, so you get a timeout error. Have you tried increasing the timeout in `future.get(timeout=0.5)` ?

Also, if you `raise e` aren't you crashing your producer ? I don't think you can expect any python client to recover if you raise an unhandled exception ;)

that date.js doesn't correctly parse relative dates, when they refer to more than 12 hours ago,,
e.g. now is Friday, May 19, 2017 6:28:00 PM,

Hi, @GraphGrail ,
To use the pre-trained word embeddings from Polyglot in your project, you can download and load it using Polyglot's Embedding class. The following examples assume that you have installed Polyglot as mentioned in https://polyglot.readthedocs.org/en/latest/Installation.html

Taking English (whose language code is `en`) as example, to download you may use the following Bash commands:
```bash
lang='en'
polyglot download embeddings2.${lang}
```

And to use it in your project, you may use the following Python snip:
```python
from os import path

from polyglot.mapping import Embedding

lang = 'en'
home = path.expanduser('~')
emb = Embedding.load(path.join(home, 'polyglot_data/embeddings2/%s/embeddings_pkl.tar.bz2' % lang))
words = emb.words
vectors = emb.vectors
```
And now you can play with `words`, which is a list of strings representing the vocabulary, and `vectors`, which is a 2-d NumPy array whose i-th row (`vectors[i]`) is the word embedding for the i-th word in `words`.
Note: To get the old behavior (when using on_delete was allowed) we should use on_delete=models.CASCADE

The behavior (iirc) is that when we click on/off, it always saves the last state to cache. When we restart or reboot, it will reuse the last state. @ricci might thought it was wrong, but maybe it's a new behavior to him because of reboots (clean state).  It updates every 20 seconds too.

Every 20 seconds...
* `xfce4-notifyd` -- it will query from the system, so I think @ricci might be mistaken.
* `dunst` -- does not really check because dunst can not be running, and still be able to notify things. For this, we are using the last known click state and will be picked last in the list.
* `lxqt-notificationd` -- it will `prep -fx`. The notification need to be running at all times for notifications to work.

If you go the unathenticated route, you will register a device upon installation and refresh token when needed. If you connect your FCM django models with users, you will register devices upon logging in and remove tokens upon logging out.

And yes, all the details that firebase needs for its operation should be entered in the firebase console.

Ah yes the situation is slightly more subtle than I thought regarding outer products, there is a good discussion in this paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/1304.6112. The place where this applies is essentially boils down to the following situation. Consider outer product ``A`` and ``B``, both of which only share indices with the rest of network ``C``. The expression is then

```python
einsum("i,j,ijk", A, B, C)
```

and if ``k>i`` and ``k>j`` then ``(AB)C`` is optimal. One can imagine a tree-like network where this might appear a lot. Some thoughts in no particular order:

1. ``'greedy'`` currently (i.e. not with this PR), it seems, only catches this when ``i`` and ``j`` are both 1 (haven't looked into why this is?):
```python
>>> from numpy.random import randn
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import opt_einsum as oe
>>> ni, nj, nk = 2, 2, 2**10
>>> A, B, C = randn(ni), randn(nj), randn(ni, nj, nk)
>>> einsum_str = "i,j,ijk->k"
```
```
>>> print(oe.contract_path(einsum_str, A, B, C, path='optimal')[1])
  Complete contraction:  i,j,ijk->k
         Naive scaling:  3
     Optimized scaling:  3
      Naive FLOP count:  1.229e+04
  Optimized FLOP count:  8.196e+03
   Theoretical speedup:  1.499
  Largest intermediate:  1.024e+03 elements
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
scaling   BLAS                  current                                remaining
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   2     False                  j,i->ij                                ijk,ij->k
   3      GEMM                ij,ijk->k                                     k->k
```
```
>>> print(oe.contract_path(einsum_str, A, B, C, path='greedy')[1])  # not-optimal
Complete contraction:  i,j,ijk->k
         Naive scaling:  3
     Optimized scaling:  3
      Naive FLOP count:  1.229e+04
  Optimized FLOP count:  1.229e+04
   Theoretical speedup:  1.000
  Largest intermediate:  2.048e+03 elements
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
scaling   BLAS                  current                                remaining
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   3      GEMM                ijk,i->jk                                  j,jk->k
   2      GEMM                  jk,j->k                                     k->k
```
... which would be easy behaviour to replicate by checking for scalars

2. I think in the most extreme case, ``ni=nj=1``, the maximum gain for a single instance is only ~2x speedup, which is not drastic - especially if one is not requesting the 'optimal' path. Once BLAS is used the difference seems to be quite minor if the few tests I've done -- would be good to see any tricky examples if you've got them though!

3. Skipping outer products could be done *only* when the expression is very large - hoping that this situation only arises towards the end of the contraction.

Interestingly `id` will work for deeper intermediates. Consider two contractions with the first three tensors being identical:
```
ab,bc,cd,defg->aefg
ab,bc,cd,def->aef
```

Steps:
```
1) ab,bc,cd,defg->aefg:   ab,bc->ac # cached
2) ac,cd,defg->aefg:      ac,cd->ad # cached
3) ad,defg->aefg:         ad,defg->aefg

4) ab,bc,cd,def->aef:     ab,bc->ac # pull result from step 1
5) ac,cd,defg->aef:       ac,cd->ad # pull result from step 2 the tensor id of`ac` is the same as above
6) ad,defg->aef:          ad,defg->aef
```

------

The other thing to consider is the "switched case":
```
ab,bc->ac   # tensor 1/2
bc,ab->ac   # tensor 2/1
```

Normal ordering should be applied to the key to watch for these cases. A simple `id(x) > id(y)` metric would work.

Could you please get latest and see if the bug appears again? I tested with two clients, both of which received updates simultaneously. Also, unpairing does not hang the app and you can then pair again.

Note that when you remove an accessory from one device, other devices will get unpaired, but will still receive updates from the accessory until the removal of the accessory gets synched among them. This is as HAP intended.

I had a closer look at the first screenshot you posted. You hit an interesting race condition: While one device was negotiating a session, another device removed the accessory from the Home and issued requests to unpair all devices that share the Home. So when the accessory needed the public key of the first device to negotiate the session, this turned out to be removed during the unpairing (hence the KeyError). This is now fixed.

Anyway, I hope this issue is resolved. Let me know :)

I found some time this morning and here is some background on the flags sent in your example

Hex/Bits for Negotiate Flags
```
32 90 88 e2
00110010100100001000100011100010
```

Flags for Negotiate
```
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_EXTENDED_SESSIONSECURITY
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_56
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_OEM
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_ALWAYS_SIGN
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_SIGN
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_128
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_KEY_EXCH
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_SEAL
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_TARGET_INFO
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_VERSION
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_OEM_DOMAIN_SUPPLIED
```

Hex/Bits for Challenge Flags
```
32 82 08 e2
00110010100000100000100011100010
```

Flags for Challenge
```
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_EXTENDED_SESSIONSECURITY
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_56
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_OEM
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_ALWAYS_SIGN
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_SIGN
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_128
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_KEY_EXCH
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_SEAL
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_NTLM
NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_VERSION
```

The NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_VERSION is defined in MS-NLMP in 2.2.2.5

> T (1 bit): If set, requests the protocol version number. The data corresponding to this flag is provided
> in the Version field of the NEGOTIATE_MESSAGE, the CHALLENGE_MESSAGE, and the
> AUTHENTICATE_MESSAGE.<24> An alternate name for this field is
> NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_VERSION.

T is the seventh bit and in your examples are set in both the negotiate message sent to the server and the challenge message received from the server. This is why ntlm-auth is trying to parse the version as it is expecting it to be there when in fact it is not.

According to MS-NLMP in 2.2.1.2 under Version

> A VERSION structure (as defined in section 2.2.2.10) that SHOULD<9> be
> populated only when the NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_VERSION flag is set in the NegotiateFlags field.
> This structure is used for debugging purposes only. In normal (non-debugging) protocol
> messages, it is ignored and does not affect the NTLM message processing.

and further down in section 3.2.5.1.1

> If the NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_VERSION flag is set, the Version field MUST be set to the current
> version (section 2.2.2.10).

As this flag is set in the reply the server MUST HAVE set the version but in your case this did not happen. My thoughts are that the proxy you are using isn't conforming to the spec appropriately and it should have sent the version while it did not.

In the end it isn't critical for this field to be parsed so I've added a check beforehand to check the length and only retrieve it when the length > 48.

- Perhaps consider also how many characters per line. If very short values, more attributes allowed.

The purpose of `go movetime` and `go depth` were to create separate modes for the engine. They were not meant to be used in conjunction. In fact the XBoard protocol explicitly states that the corresponding settings (`st` and `sd`) are orthogonal and should both be respected individually (UCI does not explicitly say so). So I would say you cannot do this according to the spec, and therefore, the API.

What you could do instead is run the engine asynchronously with one condition and blocking-wait on the future until the second condition. If you absolutely must do it synchronously, you'll have to hack the API.

Thanks for the bug report! I would be curious to hear about what you are using the package for on gitter if you have some time.

#### Serialization
The thing is that `pickle` is unable to serialize `lambda`'s and other things. And `multiprocessing` uses `pickle` underneath, so the `pack`/`unpack` trick is just to avoid delegating serialization to `multiprocessing` + `pickle`, or using something else like [`joblib`](https://pythonhosted.org/joblib/parallel.html) or [`pathos`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pathos). In the end, the same amount of data is serialized, since AFAIK `multiprocessing` serializes both the function and the data. We could partially avoid this by using threads, but other problems arise then, aka the GIL; or by using [`multiprocessing.Array`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.Array) and [`multiprocessing.Value`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.Value), which is `ctypes`-based shared memory, good for read-only access, but with their own concerns too. The documentation on multiprocessing and serialization in Python is a good start. `joblib` documentations is also good.

#### Object model for `seq`/`pseq`
Subclassing `ExecutionEngine` sounds good to me too. And maybe having something like:
```pyhthon
    def map(self, func):
        return self._transform(transformations.parallel.map_t(func))
```
Correctly implemented `Lineage` could be the same.

#### Parallel Stage Barrier
I think I get the idea. We could evaluate those blocks of `PARALLEL` functions until the next function that does not have the `PARALLEL` in its strategies, avoiding the need of `PARALLEL_BARRIER`, although the main idea of composing the functions and passing them into one single map would remain unchanged. I implemented a [proposal for that](https://github.com/versae/PyFunctional/commit/a8ecbe64255ddb8b65335f1b8bc92b6dc88a90e6). **Specifying that nodes should be ignored**

Ignoring nodes is part of a larger ticket I've been wanting to work on for some time. Have a look at https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/issues/29#issuecomment-48846170 for some thoughts about syntax and the other kinds of transforms we might eventually want to support.

Attribute grammars are interesting; I hadn't seen them before. It looks to me like Parsimonious visitors are simply an alternate spelling of them; they seem completely homomorphic. For example, the `.value` attribute in Wikipedia's example is just the entire return value of our visitor methods as typically constructed. The equivalent to multiple attributes would be returning a dict from visitor methods and passing that around.

Syntax: I've not yet found one I really like. rlib's `<`- and `>`-based syntax is clearly wrong; I've read that page many times before and still I find myself scratching my head over their assignment of semantics. [My reuse of that syntax with different semantics](https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/issues/29#issuecomment-48846170) is more memorable to me, following a physical metaphor of *squishing* a term out of existence or *expanding* a term to replace the whole expression. The downside is that there's nowhere obvious to go if we need to represent additional transformations. Your named syntax gives us that, at the cost of having to use our lexical brains. Hmm. I'd be interested in kicking around more ideas with you. Or if you wanted to implement one syntax but were able to keep it fluid in case we want to change it before landing, that'd be fine too.

Ok I have pushed the changes I mentionned in my last comment. As expected, they don't change much to the actual call to your query_* functions (in fact it even makes it a bit clearer to get rid of the `func(*args)` call).

So just to explain my code a little bit, my implementations of the different kraken api methods look like that:

```python
res = query_api('public', 'Ticker', api_params, args)
```

where `query_api(api_type, api_method, api_params, ck_args)` is the wrapper function I wrote to call krakenex. In `query_api()` I select the correct method based on the first positional argument of my wrapper function (`api_type` is either 'private' or 'public'):

```python
# just a mapping from api_type to the function to be called
api_func = {
    'public': gv.KRAKEN_API.query_public,
    'private': gv.KRAKEN_API.query_private
}
# select the appropriate method depending on the api_type string
func = api_func.get(api_type)
```

This is a bit overkill because an if/else would have done just fine, but it allows potential easy extensibility.

Then I just call the api method like this:

```python
try:
    # call to the krakenex API
    res = func(api_method, api_params)
except (socket.timeout, socket.error, http.client.BadStatusLine) as e:
    ...
```

So, TL;DR: I added my own connection handling when I call your function. I also check for other types of error messages from kraken's response.

However as you can see in my code, I only silence the connection errors and the invalid nonce errors when in "cron mode". In interactive mode, I chose to let them be errors outputted to the user.

There have been many requests to include points within the range search. I tried doing this once, but the algorithms for traversing and modifying the tree really depend on intervals containing some range. So, if you would like to include 4 in your interval tree, you could add the interval `[4, 5)`, which would start at 4 inclusively and end at 5 without including it, which is how all intervals work in the IntervalTree. They include the start value, but not the end value.

This behavior is analogous to string or array indexing:

```python
>>> s = "abcde"
>>> s[4:5]
"d"
>>> s[4:4]
""

>>> a = range(5)
>>> a[4:5]
[4]
>>> a[4:4]
[]
```

If your ranges are not discrete values, for example, they are reals with fractional parts, then for now, I would suggest coding around [`sortedcontainers.sorteddict`](http://www.grantjenks.com/docs/sortedcontainers/sorteddict.html). This is how I would like to add point support in a future release, but to a new class with a different name, since this is different in behavior from the current interval tree.

I am not involved in any of the researches that lead to this analyzer, but I've played quite a bit with it. 

Vader returns the sentiment value as 3 floating points value in range 0 <= score <= 1, plus one additional compound value that mixes all three in one value in range -1 <= score <= 1. From the README:

> The compound score is computed by summing the valence scores of each word in the lexicon, adjusted according to the rules, and then normalized to be between -1 (most extreme negative) and +1 (most extreme positive). This is the most useful metric if you want a single unidimensional measure of sentiment for a given sentence. Calling it a 'normalized, weighted composite score' is accurate.

> It is also useful for researchers who would like to set standardized thresholds for classifying sentences as either positive, neutral, or negative. Typical threshold values (used in the literature cited on this page) are:

> * positive sentiment: compound score >= 0.5
> * neutral sentiment: (compound score > -0.5) and (compound score < 0.5)
> * negative sentiment: compound score <= -0.5

> The `pos`, `neu`, and `neg` scores are ratios for proportions of text that fall in each category (so these should all add up to be 1... or close to it with float operation). These are the most useful metrics if you want multidimensional measures of sentiment for a given sentence.

Based on this, I can tell that a strongly subjective sentence will have a `compound` score close to 0, and a `neu` score close to one. From my reading of the code, the `neu` value increases as the proportion of words that match a subjective token from their lexicon decreases.

TL;DR: use the inverse of the `neu` field of the returned sentiment value (`1 - neu`). When `neu = 1`, then subjectivity score is null (0). - when `neu ~= 0` then you have the highest subjectivity score. Alternatively, you can use the inverse of the absolute value of the `compound` score (`1 - abs(compound)`).

yes, it's expected.  unfortunately it's a side effect of the cryptography.

simple-crypt uses an algorithm called PBKDF2 to process the password you give.  this is to make the security stronger, even for relatively "poor" passwords.

in short, it slows someone down who is trying to guess your password, because they, too, need to spend 3 seconds for each guess.

the trouble with the technique is that it's a compromise.  too "weak" and it doesn't protect much.  too "much" and it takes an annoying long time.  the original simple-crypt used a lower level of security, which was appropriate when it was released a few years ago now.  the latest release increased the level, because computers are getting better. 

so there's nothing i can do (sorry!).  it's just a trade-off necessary for security (i'm surprised it's so long on a new macbook pro, but it's not outside the realms of possibility - the new level is rather conservative).

Dear Xiaxiao, 
the timeout decorator is using functools for wrapping the function --> see sourcecode: 

```
# pnpnpn timeout decorator:
from functools import wraps
```
we use wrapt, what is more persistent, especially when You debug in pycharm. Also it provides better support in decorating methods of classes, static methods, etc.: 

```
# wrapt-timeout-decorator
import wrapt
```

but most important for windows is, that we use "dill + multiprocess" instead of "pickle + multiprocessing" - distinct "multiprocess" != "multiprocessing" !!!

```
# pnpnpn timeout decorator:
import multiprocessing  # implizitely imports pickle
```

```
# wrapt-timeout-decorator
# of course You need to install dill and multiprocess via pip first
# see the links further below
import dill              # the better sibling of pickle
import multiprocess      # the better sibling of multiprocessing, using dill instead pickle
```


since we dont have signals in Windows, we need to run the decorated function in a seperate process that can be terminated after the timeout occurs (You can not terminate threads).
In order to do that, the decorated function have to be "pickled" (serialized).

pickle is somehow limited, dill can serialize more types then pickle. 
This is important, because if the decorated function or method contains some elements that are not pickable, the decorator will fail with smth, like " ... not pickable "
see : https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dill
see : https://github.com/uqfoundation/multiprocess
see: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19984152/what-can-multiprocessing-and-dill-do-together

conclusion: 
#1 You want to use dill instead of pickle, because pickle doesn't support functions with closures, lambdas, or functions in main. (and many more cool things - just read the docs)
#2 multiprocessing makes some bad choices about pickling, it can not use dill. Don't get me wrong, it makes some good choices that enable it to pickle certain types so they can be used in a pool's map function. However, since we have dill that can do the pickling, multiprocessing's own pickling becomes a bit limiting.  multiprocess also adds some nice features that multiprocessing doesn't have, like multi-args in the map function.

#3 as a windows programmer You want to  use "dill + multiprocess" instead of "pickle + multiprocessing". I use it also on Linux, since I have not found any disadvantages. (although dill is a little bit slower than pickle - but the difference is negligable unless You pickle tenthousands of objects)

#4 You might try : 
```
# try to test Your programs with following imports - should pass without problems
# of course You need to install dill and multiprocess via pip first
import dill as pickle
import multiprocess as multiprocessing
```

yours sincerely

Robert

This is sklearn's GP object complaining. I believe that due to the vastly different scales between lower and upper bounds it makes it a bit harder on the GP.

You can follow their instruction and increase theta0, or increasing the nugget can help sometimes as well. To so you may simply pass these as parameters to `maximize`, which will redirect them to the GP object.

The default value for theta0 is: `theta0=numpy.random.uniform(0.001, 0.05, n_params)`

Alternatively you can either narrow down the search space, or log-transform it, something along the lines of:

    def crossValScore(log_c):
        c = exp(log_c)
        ...

and adjust the bounds accordingly.

So I went through and found that what I thought was the issue was not. Some digging has shown that Numpy, with complex types, is not happy with all varieties of NaN in the real or imaginary parts.

Easiest way to set this up is to do the following.

``` python
import numpy as np
a = np.arange(0, 2**16, dtype='uint16')
b = a.view('float16')
c = np.complex64(b)
c.imag = b[::-1]
```

Doing ``np.isinf`` or ``np.isnan`` on ``b`` or on its real or imaginary parts leads to the runtime error. A particularly interesting place is ``c[511:513]`` which is ``array([ 3.04579735e-05+nanj,   3.05175781e-05+nanj], dtype=complex64)`` which are both NaN in the imaginary part. But doing ``np.isnan(c.imag[511])`` results in no warning while ``np.isnan(c.imag[511])`` results in the warning. If we look at their bytes representations, they are

element 511: ``b'\x00\x00\xc0\xff'``
element 512: ``b'\x00\xe0\xbf\xff'``

There are three options for the hdf5storage package. One is to just ignore the warning, another is to suppress the warnings, and the other is to try to convert all such NaN's to one Numpy likes for complex numbers. I think the middle one, suppressing the warnings, is probably best.

Either way, I am going to file an issue with the Numpy package, since this is a bigger problem.

I think we want to get away from using Flask's session object--there isn't really a benefit to it and it leads to complications. Ultimately Flask's session object is outside the scope of this library and so we risk any number of unintended consequences by not bringing that scope back into the library.

What I suggest is we implement an abstraction that allows the library consumer to decide what class sessions are built with. Such objects should be compatible with Flask's sessions (i.e. present the same API) and could simply default to `flask.sessions.SecureCookieSession` to begin with. Once that's done, we should bring the entire interface into the library and use our own implementation as the default.

To be more specific (and probably clarify things), the issue here is not with the `CachedStaticFilesStorage`, it's an issue with how the static file pathes are being determined _at import time_ instead of when they need to be used.

Calling `static`, [like is done at the top of `widgets.py`](https://github.com/asyncee/django-easy-select2/blob/b9607495c73d44bcc06ff025872def6d74513ac2/easy_select2/widgets.py#L32) requires that the file that you are trying to resolve has already been collected. Because the `widgets.py` submodule is imported by `utils.py` and the `__init__.py`, the static file location will need to be determined any time `easy_select2` is referenced. Django imports all modules in your `INSTALLED_APPS` at the start of management commands (among other places), so these lines will be executed before anyone can actually try to collect the static files.

The solution requires not determining the location of the static files at import time, which in this case is as easy as not calling `static`. Django should automatically resolve the paths of the static files when the assets are rendered. Even if this is not the case, you can [dynamically define the assets](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/forms/media/#media-as-a-dynamic-property).

This really depends on the framework, and unfortunately I don't know much about Pyramid sessions. You can obtain the WSGI environment dictionary with `sio.environ[sid]`, where `sio` is a `Server` instance, and `sid` is the SocketIO session id assigned to the client in question.

The rest really depends on how easy or hard Pyramid makes it to replicate the HTTP environment from the WSGI environment dictionary. The session cookie is going to be in there.

Keep in mind that the concept of a "request" does not really apply when working with SocketIO, since there is a long-term connection with each client. What you get from `sio.environ[sid]` is going to be the WSGI environment used during the SocketIO connection.

@harmdevries89 how busy is your system? The H12 error from heroku is a timeout. Heroku does not allow any request to last longer than 30 seconds. Socket.IO is configured to send a ping every 25 seconds, and to timeout when there are no pings in 60 seconds. The 25 second one is concerning, because if for whatever delay a ping happens past 30 secs, then heroku will kill the request.

Hi @sgaseretto, specifically in your case, you have to put **blank=True** in the fields of your model that you do not want to be required. For example, the following definition of your model will not generate the **owner** field as required in the graphql mutations:

```python
class Pet(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=60)
    description = models.TextField(blank=True, null=True)  
    owner = models.ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, blank=True, null=True)
```

The EmbeddedDocumentSerializer had been designed wrong way - the `.validated_data` returned instance of EmbeddedDocument (to simplify putting it into parent document).

The right way is to follow general API of normal `DocumentSerializer`: the `validated_data` should be a dictionary of (parsed and validated) attributes, and an instance of EmbeddedModel should be created/updated during call to `save`/`create`/`update`.
This way allows to use EmbeddedSerializer separately to implement nested or complicated APIs for embedded list items. (see new test_embedded.py::TestEmbeddedIntegration)
This was the matter of my recent changes.

As for your issue #175 you probably need to take TestCase from `test_dumb.py::TestIntegration`.
And, keep in mind the difference between `serializer.data` and `serializer.validated_data`:
`data` is dictionary of serialized primitive types, ready to be jsonified;
`validated_data` is dictionary of parsed attribute values, ready to be passed to Model constructor.
Thus, ModelClass(**validated_data) creates unsaved instance of an object. Saving an instance is skipped for EmbeddedDocs.

> Are there any cases where we wouldn't want to use a squashing trie?

AFAICT, it all comes down to whether we want to access historical state. We don't need to access historical state in the middle of a transaction or block, but we do need to access historical state at the end of previous blocks.

> If not, would it make sense to use it by default everywhere?

If you mean to use it without a context, I think this would be problematic. Squashing in a context means we can be sure to delete all the intermediate state without accidentally deleting any state we need for historical access. But in general, most places that we update the trie should probably be squashed.

The answer to "Is it possible to use HTTP 1.1 Keep-Alive?" is yes.

As #71 says, if you use an Agent with a HTTPConnectionPool it will keep the configured (`maxPersistentPerHost`) number of connections open once they are idle. Connections in excess of that number will be immediately opened and closed on each request. Due to this behavior you should set `maxPersistentPerHost` to at least the number of concurrent requests you will make, or you will end up connecting once per request for each request in excess of the number of persistent connections (which as you note is really expensive for HTTPS).

Admittedly these behaviors are not ideal in a few ways:

1. HTTPConnectionPool closes connections in excess of the persistent number _immediately_. This combines poorly with a higher-level loop which makes concurrent requests one after another (say, [one which uses cooperator to limit parallelism](http://as.ynchrono.us/2006/05/limiting-parallelism_22.html)). This can be easily worked around by increasting `maxPersistentPerHost`.
2. HTTPConnectionPool doesn't provide any mechanism to limit the total number of outgoing connections, whether globally or per-host. This would be useful, since practically speaking the number of available file descriptors is a global limit, and and creating huge numbers of connections to hosts is generally rude (and may be counter-productive due to packet loss—last I knew, RFCs suggested a limit of 7 HTTP connections per origin). You can work around this by wrapping [HTTPConnectionPool.getConnection()](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/api/twisted.web.client.HTTPConnectionPool.html#getConnection) to use a per-`key` [DeferredSemaphore](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/api/twisted.internet.defer.DeferredSemaphore.html).

We could address either or both of those issues in treq, but I think that it would be reasonable to address them in Twisted itself. I've certainly encountered them when using the raw Agent API.

In any case, I'm going to close this issue because it is a question, not an issue. We'd prefer questions and discussions like this occur on Stack Overflow or the Twisted mailing list: https://twistedmatrix.com/cgi-bin/mailman/listinfo/twisted-python/ I hope that we hear from you there!

@derwolfe:

> This is a pretty large PR. I'm curious what ways there might be to break this up to facilitate easier review.

The challenge I have is that it all kinda has to fit together, so I can break it up now, but I think a reviewer would benefit from seeing all of the pieces.  But I can try if we think that's a lot easier.

> I don't mean to bikeshed, but I'm curious if there has been discussion about using typing. I've had little experience with it; some of it positive (namely it has caught some bugs for me), some of it negative (it hasn't always been as intuitive as I've liked).

Well, my intent is to add `mypy` to Klein's CI system (working through to it).  It's really useful as documentation *and* I've caught a lot of bugs because of it, so I'd be really hesitant to toss it.

I think most of the issues with it are specific to `mypy`'s implementation, which is being improved pretty steadily.

> Nearly all new functionality is marked as public API; yet very little of it is exposed via __all__. It seems like more of the new code should be marked as private (that or I'm misunderstanding how code is exposed currently :)).

Actually, it's 100% private API now, because both `_headers.py` and `_request.py` have the leading underscore and none of the symbols are exported elsewhere (yet; that will come later).

> Would it be possible or helpful for us to try to load in some of the new hypothesis tests first to target the current request API? The reason being, it could allow us to treat this as a bit more of a refactoring PR than one that completely replaces the old request.

Hrm.  I'm not excited about pouring a bunch of work into the existing API that I don't want to use…

But really the problem is that refactoring `IRequest` is way more work because of compatibility, so Glyph and I were kicking around some options for adopting this:

One was to add a flag like `Klein(newRequest=True)` which causes Klein to pass in an `IHTTPRequest` instead of an `IRequest`.  The problem here is that `IRequest` also contains the *response* headers, and then you separately return the response entity (body).  I'd like to revisit all of that, so we thought a new replacement for `Klein` (called `Router`, for example) could have routes that take separate request and response objects, etc.

This is a shockingly small change, and my feedback is all nitpicky (the feedback is really "we need a tox lint job"); I look forward to a Twisted release with the relevant bits ported so that we can move this out of `allowed_failures`.

I'm not sure I've correctly understood your solution. You want to split the multinomial feature into N separate 0/1 features and model them with a Gaussian? This might work, because in the limit everything tends to look like a Gaussian, but I'm not completely sure. 

So if it works badly, you might want to try an HMM which uses Gaussians for **some** of the features and Multinomials for the remaining features. This should be possible because the HMM allows doesn't care about the specific distribution of your features (given the state). Start with `BaseHMM` and redefine:

* `_compute_log_likelihood`
* `_generate_sample_from_state`
* `_init` 
* `_accumulate_sufficient_statistics` and
* `_do_mstep`.

This might look like a lot of code, but fear not! In fact you should be able to re-use these methods from `GaussianHMM` and `MultinomialHMM` as long as your class defines the same attributes.

If your features aren't Gaussian or Multinomial you can implement any other exponential-family distribution by redefining the same set of methods.

The problem is that extending functionality by inheriting from classes pushes
the software in a much less modular direction. For example, if I or someone else
ever gets around to writing an async version of `MatrixClient` (by subclassing
`MatrixClient`), a subclass implementing the synpase admin api wouldn't benefit
at all from that effort.

I definitely think this stuff is useful to have in the python sdk (and we won't
hesitate to add admin features for other HS as they become workable), but it
needs to be separated from the base spec matrix stuff somehow (at both
`MatrixHttpApi` level and `MatrixClient` level).

There are architectures that would make this trivial (and one in particular I'd
really like to move the sdk towards if I ever get a bit of time), but the
current architecture isn't one of them.

As a temporary solution to move us towards architectures that are easily
extendable for adding things like admin apis or other proprietary extensions to
the matrix protocol, how does the following sound?

- synapse module where all the synapse-specific code lives
- new `AbstractMatrixApi` class that `MatrixHttpApi` inherits from.
- HTTP methods for synapse admin api in subclass of `AbstractMatrixApi` within
  `synapse` module.
- New kwarg on `MatrixClient`: `admin=None`
  - If `admin="synapse"`, we instantiate both an instance of `MatrixHttpApi` and
    the admin api class.
  - If higher-level abstractions are ever written for the admin api methods,
    they would live as subclasses of `Room` and `User` within the synapse module.
    
Since this change doesn't currently touch `MatrixClient`, this is what I would
propose as the minimal acceptable change (following model laid out above).

- Create new api types such that:
  `MatrixHttpApi <: AbstractHttpApi <: AbstractMatrixApi`
  - In this model, the `_send` method would get moved to `AbstractHttpApi` while
    the rest of `MatrixHttpApi` is unchanged
- Subtype `AbstractHttpApi` with new admin api methods as `SynapseApi` or
  something, and put that code in a new `synapse` namespace.

I'll try to explain as I understood how it works.

Avro messages in Kafka have special headers to keep schema ID (https://docs.confluent.io/current/schema-registry/docs/serializer-formatter.html#wire-format) Which means `read_data()` has been called to read the message starting at the 5th byte (as we can see [here](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/master/confluent_kafka/avro/serializer/message_serializer.py#L212))

I also think that neither `read_data()` nor `schemaless_reader()` was intended to be called directly. Calling `fastavro.reader()` as in the README would call `iter_avro()` which will read header then call `acquaint_schema()`.

In my case, I was getting data from Debezium. Binlogs have a before and an after key which are identical. In the schema, the after key was a named reference to the part defined in the before key. When we call directly `read_data()`, we only get types defined in a `READERS` constant, and not the named one, until we call `acquaint_schema()` with the schema.

Since here we bypass the regular header, calling `schemaless_reader()` will call `acquaint_schema()` before `read_data()`.

I hope it clarifies my intention in this PR.

I think it makes sense to leave the contents of this message in this raw state, since that keeps them compact  - calibrated values would need to be moved to a floating or fixed point value, both of which would need to be expanded from the original full-scale range based format.  This also allows other parts of the system to perform their own calibration.

A 3.9 us time resolution sounds fine to me.

I agree that it would be overkill to send IMU settings out as part of this message.  Would settings like the full-scale range then become a system setting that can be read or written like other system settings are?

One other thing to consider is having a way to access IMU temperature.  I don't think it's appropriate to make part of this message, since 400 Hz of temperature data seems unnecessary - maybe it would make sense to have an auxiliary message that is sent at a lower rate that could include temperature and any other values that don't belong in a high rate message.

@awiddersheim 

Turns out, I'm actually passing bad parameters. First, the config I was passing, was actually the following: 

```
apiVersion: v1
          kind: deployment_config
          metadata:
              name: elastic
              labels:
                  app: galaxy
                  service: elastic
              namespace: '{{ openshift_project }}'
          spec:
              template:
                  metadata:
                      labels:
                          app: galaxy
                          service: elastic
                  spec:
                      containers:
                        - name: elastic
                          securityContext: {}
                          state: present
                          volumeMounts:
                            - readOnly: false
                              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
                              name: elastic-volume
                          command: ['elasticsearch']
                          image: 'ansible/galaxy-elasticsearch:2.4.6'
                      volumes:
                        - name: elastic-volume
                          persistentVolumeClaim:
                              claimName: elastic-volume
              replicas: 0
              strategy:
                  type: Rolling
```

Notice the above explicitly sets the security context to {}, which makes no sense. You either set attributes within the context, or don't do anything. However, setting the whole context to `null`, isn't valid. 

And, in `volumeMounts`, I explicitly set `readOnly` to `false`, which again, makes no sense. It's either True or not set. I explicitly set it to False, which is different than `not set` or `null`. 

The `openshift` client did exactly what I told it to: 

- Get the existing deployment config.
- Update that existing object with my parameters. 
- See if my modified object is different than the original. And since my parameters aren't very thoughtful, the objects end up being different.

Turns out the API is more forgiving than the our Python client.

I'm a little late to the discussion on this, but... that's never stopped me from weighing in before. ;-)

Kubernetes provides a good pattern for how we want to do health checks.

Containers can expose a healthcheck. There are two probe types that a container provides:

- Liveness: Whether the container is alive (failure = restart). Liveness is used to monitor health of existing pods.
- Readiness: Whether the container is ready to serve responses. Rediness is used to determine whether a pod's containers are all ready to handle requests.

Both probe types have the same data structure. K8s can do three types of probe:

- exec a command in the Docker contaner
- send an HTTP request and expect a 200 response
- make a TCP connection and expect the connection to be successfully established

I think we're mainly interested in HTTP probes right now, though I think exec probes will be useful for workers. An HTTP probe is pretty simple. Checks have the following major attributes:

- initialDelaySeconds: How long to wait before the first liveness probe
- timeOut: How long to wait for a response before marking unhealthy
- scheme, domain, port, path: To construct the URL to query

And that's about it. Here's an example usage from the User Guide:
``` yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  labels:
    test: liveness
  name: liveness-http
spec:
  containers:
  - args:
    - /server
    image: gcr.io/google_containers/liveness
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      timeoutSeconds: 1
    name: liveness
```
All HTTP probes are GETs (not configurable) and all expect a 200 status code (not configurable). I personally think this is fine. 

In Kubernetes, health checks are considered part of scheduling. Kublets execute health checks, and when a container fails a healthcheck, a replacement is spun up to take its place.

My opinion is that we should stick pretty close to this definition. While a methodology match would have us doing this as part of Publisher, I could see shunting this to another part of the platform purely for pragmatic reasons.

Workflow-wise, what we'd need is to handle two cases: Standard health polling and zero-downtime upgrades. Health polling is a relatively easy case. But what are the minimum number of changes we'd need to make to implement this style of health check?

## References:
* Pod health: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/blob/master/docs/user-guide/pod-states.md
* Description of Liveness Probes (exec and http): https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/tree/master/docs/user-guide/liveness
* Probe type: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/api/v1/types.go#L625
* Liveliness example: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/blob/master/docs/user-guide/liveness/http-liveness.yaml

We're in a transition period right now between Deis v1.x and an upcoming Deis v2 release.  We're fixing bugs in the 1.x branch to create an LTS release (long-term support).  For the most part, we're not contemplating adding new features there.

Specifically I believe this violates [factor no. 3](http://12factor.net/config).  By relying on configuration (i.e. non-fully qualified hostnames) that is only complete when considered in the context of the host (even though the host is a container in this instance), the application has been made less portable.

imho, the workaround you considered is actually the right way to address this.

> Just to be clear, is this correct: the client sends this value to the ipwb system after the SW has been installed, replay compares vs. current SW implementation, responds to client with some mechanism to refresh the SW code if out-of-date?

Yes, this header will be added when the SW makes the request for `fetch` call. The server needs to keep track of the latest version number of the SW (which will only be updated if the SW code changes, independent of the rest of the code changes). The same version number will be available in the SW code as a constant. If the server receives a request where that header was expected to be present, but it is not, then the server would think the request is coming from a client which does not have our SW installed. If the `User-Agent` seems like it is coming from a supported browser, then the client can be redirected to a page (preferably the home page with a note) that would install it. If the version number is smaller than the current latest then send necessary code to update the SW, preferably by redirecting to a page with a note.

Isn't it a bit odd that the `devpi` main routine is invoking 
```python

```
which [according to the spec](https://github.com/devpi/devpi/blob/6f29ed0d46eb101d6ef7f8889644954228080106/server/devpi_server/hookspecs.py#L15) is supposed to return an exit code?

Usually a project defining a hookspec is also calling it.  So this is basically a very minor convenience way of expressing a default and it blurs the line between what is a spec and what is implementation.  I think it's clearer to request from a hook calling site to provide all arguments and not have to worry cross-checking with the spec on what parameters are actually passed to impls.
For the use cases I have in mind, making the hook explicitly iterable would be fine, if not even preferred. If such hooks then don't support hookwrappers would be fine by me.

Here's my enumeration of template/merge capabilities, by ESP:

Referring to stored template by ID:
* Mailgun: not supported? (you can only supply the template content as the message body at send time?)
* Mandrill: template_name
* PostMark: TemplateId
* SendGrid: filters: templates
* SparkPost: content: template_id

"Global" merge variables--substituted into the template for *all* recipients:
* Mailgun: not supported?
* Mandrill: message.global_merge_vars
* PostMark: TemplateModel
* SendGrid: not supported?
* SparkPost: substitution_data

"Per-recipient" merge variables--within a single send call, use different substitutions for different recipients:
* Mailgun: recipient-variables
* Mandrill: message.merge_vars
* PostMark: not supported?
* SendGrid: sub
* SparkPost: recipients: substitution_data

Still need to investigate:
* "Complex" merge variables: ability to iterate over lists (arrays) and access dict (object) properties of merge variables within the template language
* "Content blocks": ability to substitute in a section of template content at send-time, and recursively merge template variables within that substituted content

That is expected. Importing paramiko before pssh stops gevent from patching the standard library before paramiko imports it. Gevent then stops you from trying to use the standard library as if it were asynchronous when it did not patch it to make it asynchronous.

There is a better way to do the above which does not break depending on import order, documentation has been updated.

```
from pssh.utils import load_private_key
client_key = load_private_key('user.key')
client = ParallelSSHClient(['myhost1', 'myhost2'], pkey=client_key)
```

I made a quick speed comparison ( [happy to save time by modifying your code from the DFT issue :-)](https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/issues/228)):

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pylab as plt
import time
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import pacf, acf

lengths = [10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5000, 10000]
t_yw = [0]*len(lengths)
t_ld = [0]*len(lengths)
t_acf = [0]*len(lengths)

n_iter = 100

for _ in range(n_iter):
    i=0
    for l in lengths:
        x = np.random.normal(size=l)
        start_time = time.time()
        pacf(x, nlags=min(len(x), 100)-1, method='ywunbiased')
        t_yw[i] += (time.time() - start_time)
        i+=1

for _ in range(n_iter):
    i=0                             
    for l in lengths:               
       x = np.random.normal(size=l)
       start_time = time.time()                           
       pacf(x, nlags=min(len(x), 100)-1, method='ld')
       t_ld[i] += (time.time() - start_time)
       i+=1 

for _ in range(n_iter):
    i=0                             
    for l in lengths:               
       x = np.random.normal(size=l)
       start_time = time.time()                           
       acf(x, nlags=min(len(x), 100)-1, unbiased=True)
       t_acf[i] += (time.time() - start_time)
       i+=1 

plt.plot(lengths, t_yw, "o-", label="yw")
plt.plot(lengths, t_ld, "o-", label="ld")
plt.plot(lengths, t_acf, "o-", label="acf")
plt.legend(["yw", "ld", "acf"])
plt.xlabel("Individual time series length")
plt.ylabel("Total runtime in seconds for {} time series".format(n_iter))
plt.show()

```
Result:
![yw_vs_ld_vs_acf](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18031651/28688965-25cb812c-7314-11e7-8dcd-3abfec66180c.png)

The feature is not that cheap, but the overhead seems to be minimal compared to `acf` which is already used in a tsfresh feature calculator. Of course, I am going to use the `method="ld"` parameter ( which enables [Levinson_recursion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levinson_recursion) instead of [Yule-Walker Equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autoregressive_model#Yule.E2.80.93Walker_equations)).

Thanks for the comments. I view this ticket as an item for discussion of a possible future enhancement, i.e., I don't think this is on the critical path for the first release. I mainly added a comment to the ticket yesterday because I was triaging tickets and thought the ticket needed a bit more clarification.

In some sense the question is, whether this kind of mode of operation (i.e., simultaneous write of multiple data streams to a single file) is something that we want encourage or discourage. There are certain operations, e.g.,  modifying, expanding, and adding objects (e.g., datasets) to files that HDF5 makes much easier than most other formats. However, with that power comes great responsibility, e.g, to manage the risks of corrupting files when we encounter errors, fragmentation of files when modifying objects etc.  One passive measure to manage those risks is establish use guidelines that avoid these kind of operations.

With regard to the specific use case of acquisition systems, it seems that in practice, acquisition system would likely want to route different data streams at least initially to different files anyways, if only to avoid possible errors, dependencies, and data corruptions. Different files could then always be linked together via a "master" HDF5 file with external links to the other files (or merged). That being said, supporting streaming data directly to HDF5 appears to be something that is on the roadmap for HDF5 (e.g, via the Single Write/Multiple Read feature https://support.hdfgroup.org/HDF5/docNewFeatures/SWMR/HDF5_SWMR_Users_Guide.pdf )

Long story short, right now this issue seems to be a bit in the abstract (i.e, this seems to be a feature that is technically possible but for which we do not have a concrete user need or use case that cannot be solved without it right now).  I think at least for now the appropriate strategy for this item may be to:

1. Add more details on iterative data write to the documentation and explicitly mention that the HDF5 write backend implemented in FORM currently handles multiple data streams by writing them sequentially. We could even add a brief discussion on why this is the case right now and describe possible alternatives (i.e., stream to separate files) 
2. Wait if a specific use-case that requires simultaneous data streams emerges and revisit this item then.

Yes it's a delicate balance to strike and by no means do I think I have it fully figured out.

I want to make it easy for the typical user, which will have only one credential and one dataset ID to worry about and won't want to deal with the rest of the mess that comes with.
If you guys think so, I'm good to go on it.

> This is easy to do currently, as your first code snippet shows.

I ... really do not think what I wrote was easy. :( 

> very few users will ever talk to multiple datasets simultaneously or require multiple connection

This will become less and less true over time. Right now people are tossing everything into "their datastore" because there was .. only one "datastore" (`from google.appengine.ext import db`). That is not going to be the case anymore, people will want to store certain data in certain regions and be able to pull from their US-based region, and put that data in their European region...

This certainly won't be "the most common thing", but I don't think that changes much.

---

> We had a massive discussion before (were you not cc'ed?):

and 

> If we need factory methods, our constructors need more work.

I definitely wasn't ... and I disagree...

DI works really well when you don't need to change something mid-flight in the dependency chain (ie, if you only ever need one connection, one set of credentials, one dataset at a time). If you need to change one piece, you have to manually create the entire dependency chain and put the final product into the function call. That sucks.

Since we do need to support multiple dependency chains in the same code at the same time... I think we need concepts to help craft all of that.

> There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.

I agree. I don't think this breaks with that. (I'm saying these aren't the same "it" -- these are two different things, each with their own obvious way to do them.)

If you need to store data (one set of credentials, one dataset, one project) the obvious way is `datastore.put()`.

If you need to interact with multiple datasets, use multiple sets of credentials, or multiple connections, the obvious way is to drill down and create things that you need.

"Proper" / expected usage won't have leaks, because it involves a context manager, which checks out the session from the pool and returns it whatever the user code does:

```python
with database.snapshot() as snapshot:
    snapshot.execute_sql(...)

with database.batch() as batch():
    batch.insert(...)
```

or has user code running inside a "managed" transaction, likewise managing the session checkout /return.

```python
def unit_of_work(transaction):
      rows = list(transaction.execute_sql(...))
      transaction.update(...)
      return len(rows)

row_count = database.run_in_transaction(unit_of_work)
```

So, the remaing issue would be with users who are constructing sessions manually.

@eddavisson Thanks. How unfortunate, however, it appears there is the intention to fix this in the future. From https://github.com/objectify/objectify/wiki/FrequentlyAskedQuestions#can-i-use-objectify-from-a-gae-flexible-runtime: 

> Can I use Objectify from a GAE Flexible Runtime?
> 
> Sort of. A little background:
> 
> For most of the history of GAE there were only Standard Runtimes. These launch one JVM per instance and connect to GAE services through a special interface called an ApiProxy. Objectify uses this ApiProxy.
> 
> Google is slowly building out REST-based interfaces to the GAE services. These perform much better when called from the Flexible Runtimes. However, the REST interface to the datastore differs significantly from the ApiProxy interface and therefore is not compatible with Objectify.
> 
> If you want to use Objectify from a Flexible Runtime, you must use the API compatibility layer that Google provides. Performance will be inferior to calling the datastore REST API or using Objectify on the Standard Runtime, but it will work. Test behavior to see if it satisfies your use case.
> 
> In the long run, Objectify will migrate to the new REST API and be usable from both the standard and flexible runtimes. However, the time for this is "not yet" - only a limited set of the GAE services are currently available via REST. Until it is possible to run a datastore transaction that enqueues a task via the REST API, the traditional ApiProxy is preferable for most applications.

I emulated the pyparsing based logic when I switched to shlex. I'm not in love with it, and am open to changes if we think it would be better.

A terminator can be used on non-multiline commands, and it will terminate the command. Stuff after the terminator is not interpreted as an additional command. Output redirection must be after a terminator (if there is a terminator present).

As a result, `load file ; !ls output.txt` is exactly the same as typing `load file`. Which is not exactly intuitive, and if tab completion works once you are passed the terminator, then it makes the user think that what they are typing will actually do something (which it won't).

In addition, the redirection tokens ('>', '>>', and '|') are treated as arguments, not redirection, if they appear before a terminator. Therefore `say hello > alice;` does not redirect any output, but `say hello > alice' does.

I guess a quick rundown of all the changes: 

run_command ends up starting bash from command line to run the nbgrader command. It's likely that you want to create an nbgrader.exe and then add it to the path.

The .replaces are to fix os.path.join. Windows (or at least mine) can understand forward slashes in the path. Backslashes are an issue with regexes and command line arguments, since the backslash escapes out. Some of the .replaces are probably unnecessary, but it might be good to just use forward slashes for everything.

The os.chmod is to allow deleting of files that are read only. The default permissions are either 666 or 644

I propose that we use labels only for a few particularly important buttons (making a bigger target that's easier to click), and for things that aren't otherwise clear. E.g. For cite2c, I want to have a 'Cite' label to highlight what the button group is, because there's no widely recognised icon for citations.

Design choices:
- Not to use the help text from the action: it's typically longer, e.g. "run cell, select below"
- Not to store the label as a new attribute on the action. Conceptually, this is related to the toolbar button, not the underlying action. Also, doing it this way makes it easy to add labels to old-style button definitions that don't use an action.

We are seeing this as well. It happens on small or large upload requests, perhaps every few hundred requests. We have added an application level retry as a workaround.

(The code snippet below enables KMS, which we also use.)

    transfer = boto3.s3.transfer.S3Transfer(s3_client)
    num_retries = 2
    while True:
        try:
            transfer.upload_file(path, bucket, key,
                                 extra_args={'ServerSideEncryption': 'aws:kms'})
            break
        except boto3.exceptions.S3UploadFailedError as e:
            if num_retries == 0:
                raise
            print('Retrying failed S3 upload.', e, file=sys.stderr)
            num_retries -= 1

@bwh1te  Bump?  Exactly the same here on clean Ubuntu 16.04.1 LTS. Also installed from pip3 (simply no python 2 preinstalled on this version of ubunty, only python 3). Same empty file in .tldr_cache, and same three empty blue lines as on SpriteMidr's screenshots.

In your example, `validate_foo` would have to be implemented as a method on a `Model` subclass, correct?  

Your counter-point makes sense, but it's problematic for me because one of my requirements is that the list of choices be inspectable from outside the model.  This is necessary for doing things like building a json-schema document or auto-generating a UI based on a model.  This would not be possible to do reliably with model-level validators, or loose validator functions passed to `__init__` because the choices would be contained within an anonymous callable. 

Then there's obviously the simplicity factor.  It's nice to just pass a callable and be done with it.  The current implementation doesn't add much complexity to the code, though I understand your POV that all these niche PRs can add up to a lot of extra cruft.

While I still like my idea, it's only fair to explore alternatives.  For example, in [marshmallow](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) *all* validation is done via [validation classes](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#module-marshmallow.validate), be it regular expression, [choices](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/marshmallow/validate.html#ContainsOnly), or min/max ranges.  There's a logic to that, but I prefer schematic's simplicity.  e.g. I prefer this:

```python
StringType(regex='[a-z]+')
```

..to this:

```python
StringType(validators=[MatchRegex('[a-z]+')])
```

And the same logic applies to choices.  Also, I don't like that with marshmallow you can provide a validator instance to a field type that doesn't make sense (e.g. regex validator to a numeric type).  

All that said, the reason I suggested using classes is that it allows us to provide even more information to a validation (e.g. human-readable labels for choices, or an option to cache callable results) while still keeping the validations inspectable (e.g. by searching for a `Choices` instance in `type_instance.validators`):

```python
class MyModel(Model):
    foo = StringType(
        validators=[Choices([1, 2, 3], labels=['one', 'two', 'three'])])
    bar = StringType(
        validators=[Choices(my_func, caching=True)])
```

So, if this PR is rejected I can always use this technique. *However*, it might be nice to provide a base `Validator` class in schematics as a convenience, and maybe even a `Choices` class with some documentation explaining the additional features. It provides both some utility and a working example of how to solve problems like this.

Anyway, no worries about letting this wait for feedback.

* IMO use numpy structured (or record) arrays
* IMO don't split up iv curves, more efficient to slice along useful dimensions, and store grouped items together
* IMO use [h5py](http://docs.h5py.org/en/latest/quick.html), transparent with NumPy, also has many compression formats

I should add that serializing and deserializing my example with h5py is trivial:

```python
import numpy as np
import pvlib
import h5py

# create some data
x = pvlib.pvsystem.singlediode(6.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026, 100)
y = pvlib.pvsystem.singlediode(5.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026, 100)

# set the dtypes to use as a structured array
my_dtype = np.dtype([
  ('i_l', float), ('i_0', float), ('r_s', float), ('r_sh', float), ('nNsVth', float),
  ('i_sc', float), ('v_oc', float), ('i_mp', float), ('v_mp', float),
  ('i', float, (1,100)), ('v', float, (1, 100))
])

# store the data in structured array, note that the IV curve is a nested
my_data = np.array([
    (6.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026,
     x['i_sc'], x['v_oc'], x['i_mp'], x['v_mp'], x['i'], x['v']),
    (5.1, 1.2e-7, 0.012, 123, 1.23*60*0.026,
     y['i_sc'], y['v_oc'], y['i_mp'], y['v_mp'], y['i'], y['v'])
], my_dtype)

# pretend that this is a grid of IV curves for matrix of (E, T)
your_data = np.copy(my_data)
# reshape my_data and your_data from (2,) to (1, 2),
# and concatentate to make fake grid
all_data = np.concatenate([my_data.reshape(1,2), your_data.reshape(1,2)], axis=0)

# output to a file
with h5py.File('THIS_IS_A_TEST_FILE.H5', 'w') as f:
    f['data'] = all_data  # key "data" is arbitrary, choose as many groups as you need
```

I just flashed a Wemos D1 Mini Pro (16mb flash) and there is no problem with the no output -n flag.

So this seems to be a problem with the driver of the Wemos D1 Mini (4mb flash).
The Wemos D1 Mini uses a different driver and shows as `/dev/tty.wchusbserial1410` 
You can only use the combination `ampy run script.py`(without -n) and afterwards view the print outputs with `screen /dev/tty.wchusbserial1410 115200`. After a while the ampy run command shows the timeout error but the program keeps running. So no problem.

The Wemos D1 Mini Pro (16mb flash) shows as `/dev/tty.SLAB_USBtoUART`
Because of that you are able to use the -n flag:
`ampy run -n script.py` and afterwards view the print outputs with `screen /dev/tty.SLAB_USBtoUART 115200`

**Hint:**
I exported the $AMPY_PORT for the ampy command to keep it short:
`echo "export AMPY_PORT=/dev/tty.SLAB_USBtoUART" >> ~/.bash_profile` (On Mac)

## Duplicate Project Names
Having multiple projects with the same name is a design feature of the DukeDS API.
This also applies to folders and files. So the API will not prevent multiple projects with the same name.

As far as I can see from [`xtensor-python/pyarray.hpp`](https://github.com/QuantStack/xtensor-python/blob/master/include/xtensor-python/pyarray.hpp), `pybind11::array` is the critical dependency and one that has received extensive changes since v1.8.x.

In the same file, you also seem to partially reimplement `pybind11::array_t`. This approach will actually break after PR #464 because of changes to converting constructors -- `array_t` was a hidden special case among pytypes. Going forward, it will be explicitly treated as such. This required internal changes in `array_t` which will also be required in `xtensor-python`'s reimplementation. Additional array changes are also planned in #338. Doing a breaking point release now, only to break it again soon after would be unfortunate.

In the short term, perhaps it would be possible for `xtensor-python` to use the stable 1.8.1 release and backport some of the `pybind11::array` methods directly in `xtensor-python`. As far as I can see from the `xt::pyarray` class, it does not require a huge amount of the new functionality, so this could be viable. I can lend a hand with this if you opt for this approach.

Long term, it would probably be best for `xt::pyarray` to inherit from `array_t` instead of `array`. Given @aldanor's planned changes for arrays, perhaps he could take a look if something specific is needed in `array_t` so that it doesn't need to be reimplemented in `xtensor-python`.
