Ahh error was a little bit deeper than I described.

The main cause of the issue was not in `array_t::ensure(src)` call with `src = None` (which actually succeeded), but in the following cast to my custom array type, derived from `array_t`, which invoked `array_t` constructor again with `object.m_ptr = 0` (casted from `None`) and this time `array_t` throws `error_already_set()` (because it assumes that if `m_ptr` is not initialized, then something bad happened).

So, the issue caused by the following line:
```C++
Array aref = Array::ensure(src);
```
where `Array` is the type, derived from `array_t`, so `array_t` constructor is called twice here, and `src` is None.

You was right about tricky maintaining of non-compositional code.

I guess there are two options for handling this:

Option 1: Always use `run_in_worker_thread` for `bind`. Let Python do whatever resolution it wants to do. This is nice because it lets us dump our pre-resolution code (at least for local addresses). Problems: this way you can't cancel `bind`, because `bind` has side-effects. This is awkward if its off in a long-running `getaddrinfo`; really for AF_INET{,6} sockets we want cancellation to take effect if it's still in the `getaddrinfo` part, which is slow and has no side-effects, but not if it's in the final `bind` part part, which is fast and has side-effects. Also, it would mean our regular `getaddrinfo` replacement machinery (#159) wouldn't work.

Option 2: keep our "pre-resolving" code, but move it internal to `bind`, and likewise for the other functions that take addresses. Run the actual `bind` call in a worker thread iff `self.family == AF_UNIX` (and the given address is not a Linux abstract address). This is a bit risky (we're trying to replicate Python's internal code for this kind of thing), but probably works fine in practice. I guess we might have to do this.

Hi guys,

I did a quick experiment with my ideas above in this repository: [nicoddemus/trio-qt-sandbox](https://github.com/nicoddemus/trio-qt-sandbox/). 

The idea of running two tasks at the same type, one running a loop which calls `QApplication.processEvents` periodically while the other executes an asynchronous function works partially, because we can't have two `trio.run` calls happen simultaneously.

But the idea of starting a task which calls periodically `QApplication.processEvents` and user actions are mapped to asynchronous functions which are then scheduled to run in the same nursery as the task with the loop, this idea seems to work.

Here's the full code for the latter idea:

```python
import trio
from PyQt5.QtWidgets import QApplication, QWidget, QHBoxLayout, QPushButton


async def loop(app, event):
    while True:
        app.processEvents()
        with trio.move_on_after(0.01):
            await event.wait()
        if event.is_set():
            return


_nursery = None


def get_nursery():
    return _nursery


def async_bind(fn):
    def wrapper():
        get_nursery().start_soon(fn)


    return wrapper


async def say_hello():
    print('Hello')


async def main():
    app = QApplication([])

    widget = QWidget()
    layout = QHBoxLayout(widget)
    hello = QPushButton('Hello', widget)
    exit = QPushButton('Exit')
    layout.addWidget(hello)
    layout.addWidget(exit)
    widget.show()

    event = trio.Event()


    def on_exit():
        print('on_exit')
        event.set()


    hello.clicked.connect(async_bind(say_hello))
    exit.clicked.connect(on_exit)

    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        global _nursery
        _nursery = nursery
        nursery.start_soon(loop, app, event)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    trio.run(main)
```

This idea works, but I don't like very much of that `move_after` wait inside the `loop` function, it feels this will make the event loop not as responsive as it should. I will see if there's a better solution in the Qt API.

Thoughts?

Crazy proposal...

Let's deprecate `globs` and `rglobs`, and make a new function called `glob`.

We can preserve `globs`'s and `rglobs`'s existing behaviour, with a deprecation warning. It forces people to actively address the change, rather than silently changing behaviour under them. We can even leave them implemented in Python.

The naming is a bit awkward, because glob and globs are very similar, and it's quite subtle. A nice thing, though, is that it would bring us more in line with Bazel's BUILD files. Bazel's `glob` behaves exactly as our new `globs`.

It shouldn't be *too* hard to write an upgrade script (though formatting python files is awkward)... For simple one-line `globs `and `rglobs`, it should be trivial, at least.

Aha, this is the thing I was complaining about the other day. You'll notice that it also re-compiles everything else too; I just ran `cargo test --manifest-path=$(pwd)/src/rust/engine/fs/Cargo.toml` and among other things it compiled sha2. Then I ran `cargo test --manifest-path=$(pwd)/src/rust/engine/process_execution/Cargo.toml` and it compiled sha2 again.

I don't entirely understand cargo's model of dependencies, but I *believe* that it does something along the lines of "compile every dep for each time it's used.

This is backed up by:
```
grep -rI "generate-grpc" src/rust/engine
src/rust/engine/target/debug/build/bazel_protos-0eb1d90210422ae2/output:cargo:rerun-if-changed=generate-grpc.sh
src/rust/engine/process_execution/target/debug/build/bazel_protos-0eb1d90210422ae2/output:cargo:rerun-if-changed=generate-grpc.sh
src/rust/engine/process_execution/bazel_protos/build.rs:  println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=generate-grpc.sh");
src/rust/engine/process_execution/bazel_protos/build.rs:  let output = Command::new("./generate-grpc.sh").output().unwrap();
src/rust/engine/fs/target/debug/build/bazel_protos-0eb1d90210422ae2/output:cargo:rerun-if-changed=generate-grpc.sh
```

Cargo is generating a fresh build script for bazel_protos for each crate which depends on it.

If we wanted to side-step the re-generation, I guess we could write the generated .rs files to a temporary directory, and only replace the existing files if they've changed... But ideally Cargo wouldn't do this to us in the first place...
I'd be in favor of fetching both .Imports and .TestImports, since `go_tests` targets would always live next to a `go_library` target, making them redundant and violating 1:1:1.

Drop-out and randomness are common techniques in deep learning to force the system to learn more robust and distributed representations. Because the system is optimizing an objective function in a supervised way, the system is forced to find solutions that can deal with the randomness. 

The HTM already has randomness inherent in a number of places and I believe these (plus the way sparsity is constructed through inhibition) already achieves the main purpose. The HTM algorithm is also not a supervised technique. An HTM with reasonable parameters that match the math constraints will be inherently robust to noise - we have tested this extensively.  I doubt adding additional noise to the input during learning will actually improve performance. However I have no problems with people trying it out.

@noobcoderT:

>  I want to use this napalm module in a python script, but not in salt cli. I want to use salt LocalClient to handle all proxy minions that running as daemon services, but I don't know all these IDs.

If your Proxy processes are already running, it's pretty easy:

```python
>>> import salt.client
>>> client = salt.client.get_local_client('/etc/salt/master')
>>> ret = client.cmd(tgt, fun, arg, timeout, tgt_type, ret, jid, kwarg, **kwargs)
```

The arguments you can send to the `cmd` function are documented at https://docs.saltstack.com/en/latest/ref/clients/#salt.client.LocalClient.cmd, e.g.,

```python
>>> ret = client.cmd('device1', 'test.ping')
>>> ret
{'device1': True}
>>> ret =  client.cmd('device* and G@os:junos and G@model:MX9600', 'probes.results', tgt_type='compound')
>>> ret = client.cmd('juniper-routers', 'net.lldp', tgt_type='nodegroup')
```

But this requires your Proxy processes to be already started. You can equally write a Python script without pre-starting them, but that's slightly more complicated, as you'll basically need to do the Proxy startup, a lighter version of this section https://github.com/saltstack/salt/blob/v2017.7.2/salt/minion.py#L3105-L3174 thus without starting Beacons or the Scheduler.

> We renamed arFour.URD to arFourFarsi.URD, but kept the UFOs in `instances_ufo/`, so the UFOs ended up having both glyphs after conversion from .glyphs to .ufo instances

I still don't understand how this could have have happened though... If one renames a glyph (i.e. changes the name attribute in that glif file, and also changes the entry in the contents.plist file), and then the UFOWriter writes the new UFO with renamed glyph on top of the existing one: the renamed glyph will have a new name, and only the glyph with the new name will be in the contents.plist; even if the old un-renamed glif file were still present in the layer folder (I'd expect it shouldn't be there any more), upon reading the UFO, the glif file which is not referenced in the contents.plist should be ignored, _as if_ it was not present in the UFO, because contents.plist is the ultimate index/mapping of glyph names to glif files, not their mere presence on the filesystem.

We need to replicate and isolate this issue in ufoLib, I guess.
In the meantime, shutil.rmtree before defcon.Font.save() is the workaround.

So any software that generates fonts from Glyphs files, should do the same. The decision is taken by the designer of the font, not the software.

A version of this is implemented in #224 . However, it differs from the original proposal:

- Rather than implementing `__enter__` and `__exit__`, I implemented a context manager method called `closing()` (similar to `contextlib.closing()`). The reason for doing it this way was two-fold:
  - Because the logic I wanted to write was easier to write using `@contextmanager`, as opposed to writing inside `__exit__`.
  - Since it is a method, it allows the caller to pass parameters that customize the behavior of the context manager.

- I also opted not to implement `__del__`. I tried it out, but it seemed to produce problems. Trying to make a network call at destruction time can, it seems, hang a program, which isn't desirable behavior. The context manager should instead be used by anyone that wants auto-revoking behavior.

Ah, I see. My preference would still be to not define equality on `EvalEnvironment` at all (just because semantically it's extremely tricky -- e.g. two `EvalEnvironment` objects might act differently if they have different `dict` objects in them, because one thing you can do with an `EvalEnvironment` is execute code that modifies stuff in the environment -- and we don't really need it.) And for tests like this, just test that the `EvalEnvironment` does in fact have the correct behavior, e.g.:

```
# Variables in the current environment that were mentioned in the formula are accessible
assert state["eval_env"].eval("foo") is foo
# But other variables in the current environment that were not mentioned, are not available
assert_raises(NameError, state["eval_env"].eval, "state")
```

(This isn't as thorough as a full equality check, but we already have thorough tests for `EvalEnvironment.subset` and `ast_names`, right? So it isn't crucial to reproduce those tests here IMHO.)

def migrate_linkintegrity_relations(context):
    """Migrate linkintegrity-relation from reference_catalog to zc.relation"""
    reference_catalog = getToolByName(context, REFERENCE_CATALOG, None)
    if reference_catalog is not None:
        for brain in reference_catalog():
            # only handle linkintegrity-relations ('relatesTo')
            if brain.relationship != referencedRelationship:
                continue
            source_obj = uuidToObject(brain.sourceUID)
            target_obj = uuidToObject(brain.targetUID)
            if source_obj is None or target_obj is None:
                continue
            # Delete old reference
            reference_catalog.deleteReference(
                source_obj, target_obj, relationship=referencedRelationship)

            # Trigger the recreation of linkintegrity-relation in
            # the relation_catalog (zc.relation)
            modified(source_obj)
            modified(target_obj)
```

                                  time_units
...
  --timestring TEXT               Python strftime string to match your
                                  snapshot's definition, e.g. 20140715020304
                                  would be %Y%m%d%H%M%S
...
```
So the default is **YearMonthDayHourMinuteSecond** with no spaces or punctuation between them.

I won't be able to look at the branch until next week but here's some quick responses:

I don't think the re/kb branch is worth saving - it's pretty much entirely superceded by the knowledge base plugins refactor, unless there's stuff there I'm forgetting about

The idea of a view vs the base is that the knowledge base should contain as absolutely little redundant data as possible - effectively the most fundemental artifacts that we can use to efficiently reconstruct anything the user would want to know about. For example, the kb would only contain the boundaries of functions, the list of basic block start addresses, and the results of indirect jump resolution, and from this we can come up with a full transition graph very quickly. In this way, the cfg and function objects become views over the knowledge base, acting as a very light cache of this simple computation. I think the rule we were throwing around is that anything that requires anything more intensive than IR translation or disassembly should go in the kb, while anything less intensive should go in a view.

The advantage of this is that it makes it really really easy to ensure there's no contradictory knowledge lying around, and any view over the old knowledge can be easily invalidated and reconstructed.

If your event handler requires it.  So, for instance:

# No event

```
# Event

```
#!/bin/bash
pane_tty=$1
pipenv_pid=$(ps -o pid= -o command= -t $pane_tty | grep pipenv | head -n 1 | awk '{ print $1 }')
if [[ -n "$pipenv_pid" ]]; then
  ps -o state=,comm= -p $(pstree -p $pipenv_pid | awk '{ print $2 }')
else
  ps -o state=,comm= -t $pane_tty
fi | grep -iqE '^[^TXZ ]+ +(\\S+\\/)?g?(view|n?vim?x?)(diff)?$'

A quick follow up to that PR. I investigated a bit further the reason why the wheel content was installed in install_purelib instead of install_platlib in my case. This was obviously due to the fact that I did not declare any extension module through the 'ext_modules' parameter of the 'setup' function from setuptools. Nevertheless I found a workaround to force the wheel content to be platlib compliant.
I simply add the following lines in setup.cfg:

[install]
install_lib=

By overriding install_lib, wheel files are no more put in the purelib folder and so the auditwheel tool is able to repair the extension modules.

I also investigated if I could switch to static libs instead of bundling shared libraries related to Tulip but it turns out it will be too painful to put in place even if it should be feasible (but a CMake nightmare). So I will stick to the way I proceed actually as it works pretty well. Allowing to repair other shared libraries with the auditwheel will be welcome but I don't want to impose my particular case, I can continue to work with my slightly patched version.

Currently, this is possible to achieve, but it's really gruesome. Using example from @kfir-drivenets :
```
import capnpy
example_capnp = capnp.load("example_capnp")

val_field = example_capnp.Example.schema.fields['val']
val_ann = val_field.proto.annotations[0]
val_range = val_ann.value.struct.as_struct(example_capnp.Range)
```

This works if we know that there is only one annotation on the field `val` and that it is always of the type `example_capnp.Range`. However if that wasn't known:
- `val_ann.name` doesn't exist. But, it is possible to match the annotation id `val_ann.id` in the list `example_capnp.schema.node.nestedNodes` and get the name from there
- to change the `val_ann.value` to a struct we need to know which struct should capnp treat it as. If we already know its name we can do `val_ann_type = example_capnp.schema.get_nested(NAME).node.annotation.type`. From here for safety, we can do `val_ann_type.which()`. If it's a struct, we get its `id` by `val_ann_type.struct.typeId` and match that again in `example_capnp.schema.node.nestedNodes` to get the name of the struct.

As you can see, it is possible to achieve, even without hard-coding names or relying in a specific order of annotations. Hopefully this will help contributors to add a nicer way, e.g. as suggested above.

> @flodolo, is this what we want? From what I've seen so far, and from what I've read in conversations in bugs, it seems that localizers read ;; to be "no valid choice in this context"?

Even English does that: we have strings starting with ";" because the message is only used for more than one element (e.g. warning on closing multiple tabs). 

I think it's better to not create an empty variant, and fall back to the default. E.g.

```properties
tabs.closeWarningMultiple = ;You are about to close #1 tabs. Are you sure you want to continue?
```

Should be migrated to

```properties
tabs-close-warning = 
        { $tabCount ->            
           *[other] You are about to close { $tabCount } tabs. Are you sure you want to continue?
        }

```

More than

```properties
tabs-close-warning = 
        { $tabCount ->            
            [one] {""}
           *[other] You are about to close { $tabCount } tabs. Are you sure you want to continue?
        }

```


Also, DevTools counter example: if they use English, you'd be creating a bunch of empty variants. With the other approach, you would be creating the first variant available, and fall back to the last one. Both are less than ideal, but the latter is definitely better.

What stands out to me is that you seem to have a fairly low rate of message
production (unless there's some other reason you can only fetch 4-5
messages at a time - are they each relatively large, say on the order of
`fetch_message_max_bytes`?). At low production rates, it's always going to
be more difficult to hide consumer fetch latency, as fetches block waiting
for more messages, and the consume thread blocks waiting for the fetcher.

Still, I agree the fetch intervals seem strangely long. Hence why I'm
wondering if you've maybe got a larg-ish `fetch_min_bytes` setting or
such...

@duilio You're right that the sequence of events you described can occur. Kafka's semantics don't offer a way to ensure exactly-once delivery without the client storing consumer offsets itself. See [this comment](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/389#issuecomment-163985917) for more information.

I'm apprehensive about giving forcing the caller to manually commit offsets during a rebalance. It can cause very weird message duplication bugs (of a kind that are actually avoidable) if the offsets are not committed, and making that behavior the default seems dangerous to me. This behavior is a result of the fact that `BalancedConsumer` contains a `SimpleConsumer` instance, so maybe that design decision could stand some scrutiny.

Ok, so the unit test is a bit misleading.  Should you instantiate a Java object from within Python, including NDArray and DirectNDArray, you will always get a PyJObject or one of it's subclasses.  Automatic conversions do not happen when you're calling a Java constructor from Python.  This enables you to get the object you specified, e.g. java.lang.Integer or java.lang.String, instead of the Python equivalent.

> Tried to trigger conversion by passing to invoke:
>    an instance of DirectNDArray
>    an ArrayList with an instance of DirectNDArray inside and calling get(0) from Python
>    an object of custom type with an instance of DirectNDArray inside and calling getData() from Python

This should be impossible if you compiled Jep with numpy support.  When you run python setup.py build you should see something like `numpy include found at somePathToPython/site-packages/numpy/core/include`.

Try checking the type of `a.get(0)` and you probably still have a Java NDArray and not a numpy.ndarray.  Your code as written works when Jep is compiled with numpy support.

In same context, a great application would be to stream `apache arrow` data through `jep`.

See http://wesmckinney.com/blog/arrow-streaming-columnar/.
As you can see in the code below, in order to achieve incrementality, a "reference" mechanism is necessary.

```
import time
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import pyarrow as pa

def generate_data(total_size, ncols):
    nrows = total_size / ncols / np.dtype('float64').itemsize
    return pd.DataFrame({
        'c' + str(i): np.random.randn(nrows)
        for i in range(ncols)
    })

KILOBYTE = 1 << 10
MEGABYTE = KILOBYTE * KILOBYTE
DATA_SIZE = 1024 * MEGABYTE
NCOLS = 16

def get_timing(f, niter):
    start = time.clock_gettime(time.CLOCK_REALTIME)
    for i in range(niter):
        f()
    return (time.clock_gettime(time.CLOCK_REALTIME) - start) / NITER

def read_as_dataframe(klass, source):
    reader = klass(source)
    table = reader.read_all()
    return table.to_pandas()
NITER = 5
results = []

CHUNKSIZES = [16 * KILOBYTE, 64 * KILOBYTE, 256 * KILOBYTE, MEGABYTE, 16 * MEGABYTE]

for chunksize in CHUNKSIZES:
    nchunks = DATA_SIZE // chunksize
    batch = pa.RecordBatch.from_pandas(generate_data(chunksize, NCOLS))

    sink = pa.InMemoryOutputStream()
    stream_writer = pa.StreamWriter(sink, batch.schema)

    for i in range(nchunks):
        stream_writer.write_batch(batch)

    source = sink.get_result()

    elapsed = get_timing(lambda: read_as_dataframe(pa.StreamReader, source), NITER)

    result = (chunksize, elapsed)
    print(result)
    results.append(result)
```

Update: We moved this file to https://github.com/CommunityDragon/Data/blob/master/patches.json

Below is the code that can be used to update it. This code could be run on a server using crontab, and the push to update the data could be done automatically (maybe using a PR with additional information about the error checking output). There should be some error checking to make sure the automatically identified timestamp is correct. Sometimes one region releases much later than the others, for example, and that will throw off the mean calculation.

```python
import arrow
import datetime
from natsort import natsorted
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path
import os

import datapipelines
import cassiopeia as cass
from cassiopeia import Region, Queue


FILEPATH = Path("$HOME/.../CommunityDragon/Data/patches.json")
FILEPATH = Path(os.path.expandvars(FILEPATH))

"""
Current thoughts on auto-updating patch info:

Check every 6 hours for updates to a Realms endpoint.
If the version is updated (and we don't have patch info for it), run the below script.
Then put the output in the patches.json file and push it.
"""


def find_patch_start_date(start: arrow.Arrow, patch_name: str, region: Region, allowable_interval=datetime.timedelta(hours=6)):
    start = start - datetime.timedelta(days=1)
    end = arrow.now()
    challengers = cass.get_challenger_league(Queue.ranked_solo_fives, region=region)

    for entry in challengers.entries:
        summoner = entry.summoner
        mh = cass.get_match_history(summoner=summoner,
                                    region=region,
                                    begin_time=start,
                                    end_time=end,
                                    queues={Queue.ranked_solo_fives})
        for match in mh:
            try:
                match_patch_name = '.'.join(match.version.split('.')[:2])
            except datapipelines.NotFoundError:
                continue
            if match_patch_name == patch_name and match.creation < end:
                end = match.creation
                print(f"New patch end time: {end}")
            patch_major_minor = patch_name.split(".")[:2]
            patch_major_minor = (int(patch_major_minor[0]), int(patch_major_minor[1]))
            match_major_minor = match_patch_name.split(".")[:2]
            match_major_minor = (int(match_major_minor[0]), int(match_major_minor[1]))
            if match_major_minor < patch_major_minor and match.creation > start:
                start = match.creation
                print(f"New patch start time: {start}")

            if end - start < allowable_interval:
                return start, end

            if match.creation < start or match.creation > end:
                break
    print("WARNING! Did not converge.")
    return start, end


def get_unknown_patches(use_versions_endpoint=False):
    with open(FILEPATH) as f:
        patches = json.load(f)["patches"]
    missing = set()

    for region in Region:
        realms = cass.get_realms(region=region)
        latest_versions = natsorted(realms.latest_versions.values())
        latest_version = latest_versions[-1]
        latest_version = ".".join(latest_version.split(".")[:2])

        if use_versions_endpoint:
            versions_latest_version = cass.get_versions(region="NA")[0]
            versions_latest_version = ".".join(versions_latest_version.split(".")[:2])

            latest_version = natsorted([latest_version, versions_latest_version])[1]

        latest_patch = patches[-1]["name"]
        if latest_patch != latest_version:
            missing.add(latest_version)
    return sorted(missing)


def update_patch_data(region_results, patch_name):
    with open(FILEPATH) as f:
        patch_data = json.load(f)
    shifts = patch_data["shifts"]
    for region, ts in region_results.items():
        region_results[region] = ts.shift(seconds=-shifts[region])

    for region, ts in region_results.items():
        print(region, ts)
    mean = arrow_mean(region_results.values())

    # Assume the patch was released at 8 AM UTC
    previous_day = arrow.get(mean.shift(days=-1).date()) + datetime.timedelta(hours=8)
    today = arrow.get(mean.shift(days=0).date()) + datetime.timedelta(hours=8)
    next_day = arrow.get(mean.shift(days=1).date()) + datetime.timedelta(hours=8)
    days = [previous_day, today, next_day]
    diffs = [abs(mean - day) for day in days]
    correct_day = days[np.argmin(diffs)]

    season = cass.Season.season_8.id

    patch = {"name": patch_name, "start": correct_day.timestamp, "season": season}
    patch_data["patches"].append(patch)

    with open(FILEPATH, "w") as f:
        json.dump(patch_data, f, indent=2)


def arrow_mean(arrows):
    mean = np.mean([dt.timestamp for dt in arrows])
    return arrow.get(mean)


def main():
    missing = get_unknown_patches(use_versions_endpoint=True)
    if missing:
        print("Missing:")
        print(missing)
        print()
        results = {}
        for region in Region:
            for patch_name in missing:
                print("{}: Finding start time for patch: {}...".format(region, patch_name))
                start, end = find_patch_start_date(start=arrow.now() - datetime.timedelta(days=100),
                                      patch_name=patch_name,
                                      region=region
                                     )
                middle = start + (end-start)/2
                print(start, end, end-start)
                print(region, patch_name, middle.timestamp)
                results[region.platform.value] = middle

        update_patch_data(results, patch_name)
    cass.configuration.settings.clear_sinks(cass.Patch)
    return


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Hi @gsemet. I also understand your point. But in this case I would definitely prefer to have an option for forcing the X11 backend. To prevent all those new issues of course this should be set to true by default. And the UI for this option should show a hint that disabling it may cause some issues.
The reason why I'm not happy with this change is that this is a step backwards for the next version of Guake. It's not just that the scaling doesn't work properly with the X11 backend. The X11 is also a very insecure protocol compared with wayland. With wayland an application can only access the content of its own windows. This is not the case for X11. Under X11 each application can access the content of other windows and also catch events that happened outside of its window(s). I'm pretty happy that currently more and more of my day-to-day used apps get adapted to support wayland so that losing this support for Guake wouldn't be that cool.
Maybe I find some time at the weekend to open a pull request at least to have an option to disable the forcing of the X11 backend.

So I'm not sure what the best approach is here, and I've played with a few today:
1. Put `matplotlib.use('agg')` right after `import matplotlib`
    It has to be that early because `import matplotlib.testing.decorators` triggers an import of `pyplot`, which will lock the backend setting. I feel a little odd of locking in a particular setting disallowing pdf, ps, etc.

2. Add a `'backend'` kwarg to `mpl_image_compare` and use `pyplot.switch_backend` "experimental" functionality.

Hello,

Setting sync standby names to '*' means that any replica can be selected to
be the sync standby.

In the case where there were two available this would mean that Patroni
potentially no longer knows which standby is selected (as it hasn't been
enforced with an explicit setting yet) and therefore can't failover with
guaranteed zero data loss at that time.

This patch (for me anyway) is about trying to enforce zero data loss on
failure of the master. Using * would not achieve this.

Now I think about it though I also need to modify the startup code so it
always started up with the dummy host set as well - else writes could be
lost till a sync standby was selected.

Cheers,

notifications@github.com> wrote:

> *@CyberDem0n* commented on this pull request.

>
> In patroni/ha.py
> <https://github.com/zalando/patroni/pull/400#discussion_r102448809>:
>
> > @@ -242,6 +245,11 @@ def process_sync_replication(self):
>                      if not self.dcs.write_sync_state(self.state_handler.name, None, index=self.cluster.sync.index):
>                          logger.info('Synchronous replication key updated by someone else.')
>                          return
> +
> +                if self.is_synchronous_mode_strict() and picked is None:
> +                    picked = 'patroni_dummy_host'
>
> I still think it would be better to have a '*' there and I will explain
> why.
> First of all let's remember how synchronous_mode works:
>
>    1. Patroni picks one of the streaming replicas (closest to the master)
>    2. It sets this replica as synchronous in the postgresql.conf and runs pg_ctl
>    reload
>    3. Sleeps for a couple of seconds
>    4. Verifies that chosen replica is really became synchronous
>    5. Publish this replica into DCS
>
> If the step 4 didn't succeeded it will continue with HA loop and return to
> the task of choosing and setting synchronous replica only on the next
> iteration.
>
> So Patroni doesn't have a goal to block writes on the master until
> synchronous replica is published in DCS.
> During loop_waitseconds there is a chance that some other replica will
> connect, but it will have no chances to become synchronous until the next
> iteration...
>
> If the synchronous_standby_names is set to '*', replica will connect and
> become synchronous and Patroni will need to go only through steps 1, 2, 5.
>

Alright, there's a cleaner way to fix it which is that every time a new array is stored (not just the first time), `NetCDFMonitor` should check if the dimensions are compatible (if there are any new dimensions), and alter the array to be compatible if it must do so (by adding new dimensions). We're not using xarray, we're using `netCDF4` as a backend directly for that component. However, xarray (and `netCDF4`) do allow changing array shapes/dimensions by replacing the variable with a new variable and copying the data over.

This would be fully compatible with `write_on_store`, the write operation would just be more expensive any time new dimensions are added to arrays.

At this point I agree that full arrays should be returned by `get_default_state`. But I think `NetCDFMonitor` should be fixed to work even when it's given sparse arrays that are filled out later. I'll open an issue in `sympl` for this.

Yes. SoS recognizes that workflows typically have a large amount of "connection" code or "light analysis" that do not need to be executed as tasks, so it makes clear that **only tasks are external** and everything else should be executed by SoS. Other workflow systems seem to treat everything as tasks and provide very limit amount of glue code (correct me if I am wrong here), and I frankly do not know how snakemake handles small jobs --- clusters are not designed for them.

The consequence is that we typically run workflows locally, even interactively in Jupyter, and the workflow would submit large jobs to remote hosts and queues. The workflow could wait or exit after the jobs are submitted. (#448). We could run a sos workflow on the head node if the workflow has all tasks, but generally speaking sos workflows are not suitable for headnode execution.

All the RealSense sensors are a bit different so I'll only talk about the SR300 here.

- Frame timestamps are indeed generated on the camera using an on board hardware counter (not a real time clock or anything fancy; but we definitely try to avoid OS timestamps as much as possible)
- The first few pixels of each image are actually data with this counter value (and they periodically overflow, so the software needs to track them as well)
- The RGB and IR imager are not exposed together and have highly independent framerates (depending on filter settings, exposure, etc)
- The purpose of the librealsense sync.cpp is to provide to provide sets of depth/ir/rgb frames with similar timestamps
- The SDK (and future versions of librealsense) will provide a working version of HW sync, which SR300 supports
- HW sync depends on some per-frame metadata structs as part of the UVC protocol. These are easy to access on Windows, but require yet more kernel patches on Linux. This is presently not implemented in our library.
- HW sync actually does attempt to trigger the imagers at the same time, and provides per-frame (via the struct) into about timing and exposure.
- Depth Control Presets only affect the R200 which you've already noted
- The RealSense SDK does provide a good amount of documentation on the available camera controls (https://software.intel.com/sites/landingpage/realsense/camera-sdk/v1.1/documentation/html/property_device_pxccapture.html) but librealsense sometimes has different identifiers for them.
- The SR300 is not per-unit calibrated, and so all calibration info (for all resolutions, I think) is based on a model. It _might_ be possible to get a better color camera calibration manually, but I don't recommend it.
- The nominal/best resolution for SR300 is 640x480 @ 60fps. The color imager is capable of 1080p @ 30fps. 
- Any data related to accuracy is highly dependent on both camera settings and environment/object

Below is the rough calculation :
When device is not streaming but sensor is opened, roughly consuming : 0.2W

That doesn't sound right. I'll look into the issue (maybe CSC is inefficient compared to CSR?) but in the meantime, here's some code that worked for me and finished in half an hour:

```python
f = h5py.File("/Users/sten/10X_datasets/1.3M_neurons/1M_neurons_filtered_gene_bc_matrices_h5.h5")
genes = f["/mm10/gene_names"][:].astype("unicode")
accessions = f["/mm10/genes"][:].astype("unicode")
indptr = f["/mm10/indptr"][:]

def read_n(i, n):
    if i + n > f['/mm10/indices'].shape[0]:
        n = f['/mm10/indices'].shape[0] - i
    cells = np.zeros((genes.shape[0], n))
    for ix in range(i, i + n):
        a = indptr[ix]
        b = indptr[ix + 1]
        cells[f['/mm10/indices'][a:b], ix - i] = f["/mm10/data"][a:b]
    return cells

i = 0
ds = None
while True:
    cells = read_n(i, 1000)
    print(i)
    if ds is None:
        loompy.create("10x_1.3M_cells.loom", cells, {"Gene": genes}, {"CellID": np.fromiter(range(i, i + 1000), dtype='int')})
        ds = loompy.connect("10x_1.3M_cells.loom")
    else:
        ds.add_columns(cells, {"CellID": np.fromiter(range(i, i + 1000), dtype='int')})
    i = i + 1000
```

You'll have to change the file path of course. Also, I didn't bother about the 10X cell barcodes, but if you want them, you'll have to pass them instead of the `np.fromiter(range(i, i + 1000), dtype='int')` both when creating the initial file and when adding columns.

Maybe a few words on the rationale of this change?

I was thinking about that just these days and probably a good (but maybe too abrupt) refactory, would include having a common standard interface to `nd_filters` where every cheaply generalizable algorithm is written as: `nd_filter_1_1`, `nd_filter_n_1`, `nd_filter_n_m`, `nd_filter_n_x`, where the first number indicates the number of inputs and the second number the number of outputs, and the `x` in `nd_filter_n_x` indicate that the number of output must be inferred from the input. Note that the `_1_1`, `_n_1`, etc. suffix do not need to be present in the function name, but it would be good practice to have this piece of information written somewhere in the documentation; also, most filters would probably by `_1_1`.
Additionally, all `nd_filters` should have a keyword argument somewhere in the signature to indicate whether the last dimension has to be interpreted as separate channels.
I would probably go as far as adding the (maximum?) dimensionality of the input in the function name if it does not accept an arbitrary number of homogenous dimensions, or a certain algorithm behaves differently depending on the dimensionality. Hence, XD-only, filters should be written as e.g. `my_beautiful_filter_2d`, `my_beautiful_filter_3d`.
If a specialized implementation of the algorithm exists for speed reasons at lower dimensionality, this should be accessed automatically by the `ND` version.

libgfortran errors are because we had to update our compiler to fix some mathematical errors that were showing up in scipy.  Many packages in our system do not appropriately pin libgfortran to the older version, regrettably leading to the current situation: the newer libgfortran breaks old packages.

You can try either:
- updating your software to use Numpy 1.10 or above (in your conda-requirements.txt).  All numpy 1.10 builds should use the newer library, which should be a dependency where necessary (not sure actually why it isn't a dependency for you already)
- adding libgfortran ==1.* to your conda requirements

Regarding the automatic updating, there has been a lot of discussion about changing this, but no traction yet.  One very important difference between conda 3.x and 4.x is that 4.x no longer allows inconsistent package dependencies.  This is both good and bad.  It means that when things are built properly and dependencies line up, things work more reliably.  It also means that environments that used to work (sometimes suboptimally) may no longer be installable, with unsatisfiable dependency issues.

> I do not have these problems with apt.

It sounds like you have a solution here?

Anyone in the world is free to create channels and upload packages, unfortunately they are free to make their own decisions based on what they know about about binary compatibility.

Even for those who do understand these details, the availability of toolchains in the past has been a big problem with conda-forge and bioconda using either system compilers or RedHats devtoolset. We found both these options inferior as they prevent us from building C++14 and C++17 software and we also use pseudo cross compilers to ensure incompatible system libraries do not get linked to our software.

So you need to stick to either Anaconda Defaults (and build the packages you are missing against Anaconda Defaults packages) or you need to stick to using a purely conda-forge and bioconda environment.

This is something we are trying to fix, the hope is to switch bioconda and conda-forge over to using our compilers. We already consider conda-forge our upstream and our recipes are forked from there so we are definitely on the right path.

> I just tried a variety of channels and channel precedence. All produced the same error.

As I said, you won't be able to mix, so pick AD and build the missing packages yourself, you can even upload them to a free channel on https://anaconda.org for sharing with colleagues (I can assist with R packaging if you wish) or pick conda-forge and bioconda.

@dperetti @codekiln in GraphQL all `ID` types are represented as strings: https://graphql.org/learn/schema/#scalar-types

> ID: The ID scalar type represents a unique identifier, often used to refetch an object or as the key for a cache. The ID type is serialized in the same way as a String; however, defining it as an ID signifies that it is not intended to be human‐readable.

graphene-django has made the decision that Django ID's should be mapped to the GraphQL concept of ID's and I think that is the right decision. You can always work around it like @codekiln has by explicitly mapping the Django field to a graphene Int field:

```python
class UserType(DjangoObjectType):
    id = graphene.Int(source='pk')
```

Okay. We just need to figure out a clean way to import snippets, if we want to use it both for "things to copy/paste into your code if you need them" and "things we need in multiple recipes and don't want to copy/paste".

1. Require people to add the `snippets` path to their `PYTHONPATH`? (That's a bit cumbersome.)
2. Have the examples do silly things like `sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '..', '..', 'snippets', 'foo')`? (That means they cannot simply copy an example somewhere else and modify it.)
3. Have the examples import the snippets via the `imp` module? (Same problem as for 2.)
4. Include relative symlinks to the snippet files that are used? (That probably won't work on Windows.)

Other ideas? It's probably just a few things like the MNIST data download/reading code and maybe some layer implementations that could be shared between multiple tutorials or papers and tutorials, but still.

> Are there any edge cases I'm not thinking of

Yes, the user could have passed a named variable for `W_hid_to_hid`, say, "recurrent weights", and you'd turn it into "recurrent weightW_hid_to_hid" now :)
Instead, you should give a name to the `DenseLayer`s you are constructing, so they will name their variables accordingly if they don't have a name yet. Something like:
```python
basename = '' if 'name' not in kwargs else (kwargs['name'] + '.')
in_to_hid = DenseLayer(..., name=basename + 'input_to_hidden')
hid_to_hid = DenseLayer(..., name=basename + 'hidden_to_hidden')
```
This would give you `layer_name.input_to_hidden.W` and `layer_name.hidden_to_hidden.W`, which would also match how they can be accessed through the `CustomRecurrentLayer`.

I agree that `layer_name.W_in_to_hid` and `layer_name.W_hid_to_hid` would be nicer, but then you'd need to create the weight parameters using `lasagne.utils.create_param` inside `RecurrentLayer` and pass them to the `DenseLayer`s:
```python
basename = '' if 'name' not in kwargs else (kwargs['name'] + '.')
W_in_to_hid = create_param(W_in_to_hid, (int(np.prod(input_shape[2:])), num_units), basename + 'W_in_to_hid')
b = create_param(b, (num_units,), basename + 'b')
in_to_hid = DenseLayer(...)
...
```
That sounds like a lot of effort just for the sake of getting good variable names, but it's still less hackish than the third alternative: Check if `W_in_to_hid` had a name before passing it to `DenseLayer`, and only apply your name hack if it hadn't.

Pick whichever you like best. I think I'd prefer the first. (And make sure to amend your commit rather than adding a new one and squashing it afterwards, to save electricity! :) )

I think a better strategy would be to create a sister library for TensorFlow, that provides largely the same API as Lasagne (where possible), but returns TensorFlow objects where Lasagne would return Theano objects. That would make it much easier for users and devs who only care about one or the other. Of course we'd need a few devs involved in both of them, to keep the APIs roughly in sync. But it would of course be fine if they didn't support exactly the same stuff.

The main thing I would want to avoid is for it to slow things down. If we commit to supporting everything on both backends, suddenly implementing a feature is twice as much work. I think that would be a bad idea.

It's a template for a client. In the main we always pass those arguments, sometimes it might be needed, and othertimes not. The import of the different functions as main needs to be able to import different functions, and pass on the same things. In this case, the arguments and parser. It doesn't hurt to have it, and it goes with the logic of the client. In retrospect there are other ways to do it, but I chose this one because I liked it.

> Maybe it is my lack of python knowledge showing, but for cli it seems to not exist in the scope where it is used (the python function), which seems like it should be a problem. 

Not really, you can define a function, or do an import, wherever you like. As I said, the location is important because we need the init of the client to happen **after** the primary client executable. If you put the import at the top, it gets called when it's imported at the top of the main client function (and the logic is thrown off).

> I don't understand why it isn't. Even if it isn't a problem, I would ask if it would be possible to maybe make it more obvious what is happening, perhaps by making cli act in a less surprising fashion - e.g. have an initialization function that can be called at the appropriate place? 

This is exactly what we have - the function is called "get_client" :)

> That said I haven't looked at cli yet, before making assumptions, but this would be my general expectation.

Maybe it would be good to trace execution, and try some of these changes on your own and see the result. For example, the client needs to be able to do both of these:

```
SREGISTRY_CLIENT=docker sregistry...
sregistry pull docker://
```
For the first, we get the environment setting before anything is called. This would work for a more global import. For the second, we don't get the client until **after** parsing arguments. A global import would have already been called (and a different client backend chosen, singularity hub and not docker).

> I put some code to only process up to 10 tasklets in a row before breaking out and trying doing some other things (timers, send/recv, ...). But can you explain a bit more why OpenThread needs to sit in a busy loop, polling? 

To the extent that something is using tasklets to implement polling for a condition (which, with your loop, results in a busy-wait), I believe that is a bug that should be fixed. However, I don't think that is the bug we are seeing here.

I think the real problem is in how we dispatch tasklets. Ideally, at each pass through the main loop we would dispatch all tasklets that have been added *up to that point*. Any new tasklets added as a part of dispatching those tasklets would be handled at the next iteration through the main loop. This prevents the other parts of the main loop from being starved of cycles. Unfortunately, I don't think the current API allows you to implement that sort of behavior.

Perhaps we need to adjust the API to allow that?

Okay, I understand the distinction now.  OpenThread's current implementation will transmit a given packet up to 16 times and, if no ack is received, the link will be invalidated.  The Thread spec implies that it should take 4 distinct packets (each with 4 MAC transmissions) before the link is invalidated.  Concretely, if an application is sending packets with a period of X seconds, then OpenThread will invalidate a link on the 1st application packet whereas the Thread specified behavior would invalidate the link after the 4th application packet (3 * X seconds).  Yet another behavior that is not explicitly tested in the cert test plan.  I'm fine with updating `kMaxFrameRetries` to 3 to be inline with the spec.
It's really a good idea to store the frame counter value x ahead, I will update the PR with this mechanism, and will also address other inline comments, thanks.

Ok, the pull request #122 adds two main classes, the `TinyDbObserver` and the `TinyDbReader`. 

The `TinyDbObserver` behaves very similarly to the `MongoDbObserver`, having the same support for serialising datetime objects, numpy arrays and pandas objects. To do so it makes use of the [tinydb-serialisation](https://github.com/msiemens/tinydb-serialization) extension to TinyDb. It is also paired with the HashFS library as mentioned for file storage.  Together these allow for 'serialising' a file handle object to the database, which I have added for convenience to the file data structure in the sections `experiment, sources`, `artifacts` and `resources`. 

To aid in getting the data out again, the `TinyDbReader` sets up the necessary connections to the hash file system and handles serialisation from the metadata.json. In addition it allows searching experiments either by index, by experiment name, or by using the TinyDB API.  It has methods to return the metadata, the file handles for the files, or a rendered text based summary report based on the information searched for.

With `continuous_update` set to `False`, the view only sends update to the model when the browser sends the `change` event. With `continuous_update` set to `True`, the view sends updates to the model in response to both `change` and `input` events. The difference between these is described thoroughly [here](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Events/change).

When @jasongrout  implemented `continuous_update`, he rationalized defaulting to `False` for text boxes as (PR #1545):

> I default continuous_update to False for the textboxes because I figured if people were typing, that's typically what is wanted.

That makes sense for inputs which are purely text, but maybe it makes less sense for inputs which also allow mouse interaction, like `{Float,Int}Text`.
(i.e., make sure the views can deal with those two values being undefined)

Hey @EmmaZhu

> I followed instruction : https://www.microsoft.com/net/core#linuxredhat to install .Net Core. Seems I need to enable dotnet with "scl enable rh-dotnetcore11 bash" whenever I started a new bash session. Is this behavior intended?

Yes. There's a few reasons for use-via-`scl`. The most important one is that .NET Core requires certain libraries that are just too old on base RHEL. In particular, some networking API requires features that are not a part of libcurl on base RHEL. So we build a modified version of libcurl that only gets activated via SCL. This lets all programs on the system continue working without being aware of the modified libcurl but .NET Core can see it and make use of more advanced features that part of the networking API in .NET Core needs.

Another reason is that without SCL, we would be tied to RHEL's release cycle and not be able to get updates out within days or weeks of upstream release.

> I know I can put the command in .bashrc. I just think it'd be much convenient if the installation can enable dotnet automatically.

Please feel free to file bugs at bugzilla.redhat.com. User pains help us direct effort and weigh options. It's kind of hard for us Red Hat folks to monitor all github issues :)

We have only tested this with Puppetdb 5.1.

The documentation that I referenced includes the same exact example for puppetdb versions 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, 5.0, 5.1, and 5.2:
https://puppet.com/docs/puppetdb/4.2/api/query/curl.html#querying-puppetdb-with-post

The example specifically shows sending a POST with curl, and putting the query into the body. 

I just found this documentation (for version 4.2 and version 5.2) that specifies that POST queries should have the query as a JSON encoded payload (i.e., in the body):

https://puppet.com/docs/puppetdb/4.2/api/query/tutorial.html#how-to-query

> Queries are performed by issuing an HTTP GET or POST request to an endpoint URL and specifying a query URL parameter (in the GET case) or a JSON-valued payload in the POST case, which contains the query to execute.

> To implement the LSTM for vf, Should I have to handle states of model / vf_model attributes seperately?

I think you're right, that's needed if you use two different sets of placeholders for (state_in, state_out, seq_len). However if you use a "combined model" that produces both logits and vf outputs from a single set of (state_in, state_out, seq_len), then it can be used in rllib out of the box.

The simplest solution is to share layers, e.g. the following works:

```
--- a/python/ray/rllib/agents/ppo/ppo_policy_graph.py
+++ b/python/ray/rllib/agents/ppo/ppo_policy_graph.py
@@ -161,8 +161,9 @@ class PPOPolicyGraph(TFPolicyGraph):
             vf_config["free_log_std"] = False
             vf_config["use_lstm"] = False
             with tf.variable_scope("value_function"):
-                self.value_function = ModelCatalog.get_model(
-                    obs_ph, 1, vf_config).outputs
+                from ray.rllib.models.misc import linear, normc_initializer
+                self.value_function = linear(
+                    self.model.last_layer, 1, "value", normc_initializer(1.0))
             self.value_function = tf.reshape(self.value_function, [-1])
         else:
             self.value_function = tf.zeros(shape=tf.shape(obs_ph)[:1])

```

Actually the above is how rllib implements value functions in a3c and impala. Probably PPO should do it in the same way. To do more complex things, you can also define a custom LSTM model.

> How does env_runner generate states for the model? How can I adjust it to the vf_model?

It will generate the initial state using get_initial_state(), pass it in using state_in, and get it via state_out. However as noted it only works for a single model, not multiple

---

Just for reference, originally I was thinking the following would be sufficient (it isn't, because we assume only one set of placeholders for state in/out):

```
--- a/python/ray/rllib/agents/ppo/ppo_policy_graph.py
+++ b/python/ray/rllib/agents/ppo/ppo_policy_graph.py
@@ -159,10 +159,9 @@ class PPOPolicyGraph(TFPolicyGraph):
             # mean parameters and standard deviation parameters and
             # do not make the standard deviations free variables.
             vf_config["free_log_std"] = False
-            vf_config["use_lstm"] = False
             with tf.variable_scope("value_function"):
-                self.value_function = ModelCatalog.get_model(
-                    obs_ph, 1, vf_config).outputs
+                self.vf_model = ModelCatalog.get_model(obs_ph, 1, vf_config)
+                self.value_function = self.vf_model.outputs
             self.value_function = tf.reshape(self.value_function, [-1])
         else:
             self.value_function = tf.zeros(shape=tf.shape(obs_ph)[:1])
@@ -191,9 +190,9 @@ class PPOPolicyGraph(TFPolicyGraph):
             action_sampler=self.sampler,
             loss=self.loss_obj.loss,
             loss_inputs=self.loss_in,
-            state_inputs=self.model.state_in,
-            state_outputs=self.model.state_out,
-            seq_lens=self.model.seq_lens,
+            state_inputs=self.model.state_in + self.vf_model.state_in,
+            state_outputs=self.model.state_out + self.vf_model.state_out,
+            seq_lens=self.model.seq_lens + self.vf_model.seq_lens,
             max_seq_len=config["model"]["max_seq_len"])
 
         self.sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
@@ -244,4 +243,4 @@ class PPOPolicyGraph(TFPolicyGraph):
             self._loss, colocate_gradients_with_ops=True)
 
     def get_initial_state(self):
-        return self.model.state_init
+        return self.model.state_init + self.vf_model.state_init

Once a process using TensorFlow starts to use space on a GPU, it never releases it until the process is killed (see #616), so the actor will continue to occupy space on the GPU until the actor is killed. The actor will naturally be killed when the actor handle goes out of scope in Python.

E.g., if you do

```python
actor = CNN1.remote()

del actor
```

Then the call to `del actor` will send a task to the actor that will cause the actor to kill itself when the task runs.

Hi, @perryism! As not all accounts have billing info, the intended way to delete a `BillingInfo` is to retrieve the billing info from the server through the `Account`, then delete it. The `BillingInfo` then knows what its own URL is already, so the `member_path` URL template isn't necessary.

    acc = Account.get(account_code)
    try:
        billing_info = account.billing_info
    except AttributeError:
        # The account already doesn't have a billing info.
        pass
    else:
        billing_info.delete()

The account code isn't an identifying property of the `BillingInfo`, but rather the account it belongs to, so using `member_path` in this way didn't seem appropriate.

As you've seen, this makes it a little more complicated than it should be. It does seem like you should be able to get a `BillingInfo` directly by account code. We'll look at making it easier to go right to the `BillingInfo` in the future, but for now this is as intended. Thanks!

Pillow is a hard dependency of imageio, and it is the preferred plugin for all formats except tiff. I suspect that forcing imageio to use pillow is thus also not necessary, but if I am proven wrong I am happy to add support for that. At the moment one can force specific formats, e.g. `imageio.imread(filename, 'png-pil')`, but not a plugin (i.e. a collection of formats).

As for conflicting keyword arguments for formats that support the same kind of files. Yeah, that could happen, but I guess its rare. That's why its possible to explicitly use a specific format.

The difficult with the `log(x-y)` is that `log(0)` is undefined.  This is why I go through the gyrations i do with the price comparator. Unfortunately, we can't really do the same thing since prices is a ratio variable but time is only an interval variable (see http://www.graphpad.com/support/faqid/1089/ for what this means, but basically we can say that one price is 50% higher than other price, but it doesn't make any sense to say that may 15, 1957 is 50% later than jan 1, 1852.

So... since, we are going to have zeros.  we can't really use logs.  Now let's get to your first question. The reasons that I *wanted* to use logs is the intuition that the difference between 3 days and 2 days is, in some sense, more important than the difference between 1000 days and 999 days even though in both cases the differences between the respective values is just one day. 

There are other ways to do this, for example power transforms.. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_transform

cnn:

dynet:
explicit MomentumSGDTrainer(Model* m, real e0 = 0.01, real mom = 0.9, real edecay = 0.0) :

This PR is especially useful for svg optimization where image contains a lot of curves. This PR is also useful when the svg optimization can benefit from low precision like 1 or 2 without loss of integer precision.

I've noticed that curveto [C and S] path sections do not much change the appearance when the curve control point precision is greatly reduced. On the other hand, changing the (X,Y) end points or anchor points to the same degree causes noticeable distortion to the image.

"--set-precision 0" is just like "--set-precision 1" but allows for better conversion from curveto into lineto
So for example
c0.123 0.223 0.323 0.423 55.6 6.7

with precision 1 (wont collapse to lineto)
"c.1 .2 .3 .4 60 7" 

with precision 0 (will collapse to lineto)
c0 0 0 0 60 7

"--keep-int-precision" This is an option I've always wanted. I've noticed that doesn't improve file size to reduce precision on integers.
example: take 12345.67 and 345.25
with precision 1 results: 10000 and 300
with precision 3 results: 12300 and 345
with precision 5 results: 12345 and 345.25

with --keep-int-precision option
with precision 1 results: 12345 and 345
with precision 3 results: 12345 and 345
with precision 5 results: 12345 and 345.25


So using the popular "kiwi.svg" compression example, i get an extra 43% reduction no visible distortion
--set-precision 5 (default) [RESULT 1956 bytes]
--set-c-precision 0 --keep-int-precision --set-precision 3 [RESULT 1112 bytes]

(above results combined with --strip-xml-prolog  --strip-xml-space --remove-metadata --indent=none --enable-comment-stripping --enable-id-stripping --enable-viewboxing)

The only side effect i see is if the user sets the --set-precision 6, control points will still be at precision 5. The user must explicitly set the control point precision, if the --set-precision is above the default 5. Setting below default is not a problem.

Okay, so it appears that we're logging the encoded `post_data`: https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/f948b8b95b6df5b57c7444a05d6c83c8c5e6a0ac/stripe/api_requestor.py#L334-L335

This works fine for regular x-www-form-urlencoded requests, but not so well for multipart-form requests.

I think we ought to log the params before encoding. It's what we do in the Ruby library: https://github.com/stripe/stripe-ruby/blob/d41a59fc82cd9989b16ebad9a4bcd9e39395466e/lib/stripe/stripe_client.rb#L443-L446. Files would simply be represented by their usual Python's `repr`, e.g. `<io.BufferedRandom name='/path/to/file.jpg'>`.

set ws=wscript.createobject("wscript.shell")
ws.run "ssserver.exe -p (Port) -k (Password) -m (Encryption) /start",0
or
ws.run "ssserver.exe -c shadowsocks.json /start",0

And create a new task scheduler for this **.vbe **or** .vbs **.
Set "at startup"and"run whether user is logged on or no".

Put this .vbe into the same folder and ssserver.exe.
Example:"**C:\Python27\Script\**".

You can create a new config file  **shadowsocks.json** in the same folder.
**shadowsocks.json**'s content:
{
    "server":"my_server_ip",
    "server_port":8388,
    "local_address": "127.0.0.1",
    "local_port":1080,
    "password":"mypassword",
    "timeout":300,
    "method":"aes-256-cfb",
    "fast_open": false
}

- The documentation [1] is correct by stating 

  > The callable should return a **list** of accepted content types

  Here, @bomb-on tried to return a **single** internet media type **as scalar**, hence the misbehavior of Cornice
- However, when specifying the list of accepted content types as plain value, Cornice allows lists **and** scalars

To improve the functionality:
- Make content_type checks using callables capable of returning single internet media types as scalars, see #350

Thinking about this some more, I'm starting to think that Option 5 is just a less-good version of Option 1. The docs have two ways to grab the highest-precedence ProxyPass:

a> The configured ProxyPass and ProxyPassMatch rules are checked in the order of configuration. The first rule that matches wins.

b> In contrast, only one ProxyPass directive can be placed in a Location block, and the most specific location will take precedence.

In other words, you can be the first ProxyPass (a), or be a ProxyPass inside the most specific Location block (b). Option 1 chooses to be a ProxyPass inside the most specific Location block, and simply turns off ProxyPass and RewriteEngine. That should be at least as good as Option 5.

It may also be possible to use an even more specific Location block, like `Location /.well-known/acme-challenge/acb1358gjkdsfdsf`, and have a directive to return a certain literal string. I had this exact same problem, found a difference in staging vs production. The staging env works fine:

`certbot certonly --webroot --webroot-path /var/www/html -d foo.com --staging` 

And without `--staging` certbot throws an error:

`Failed authorization procedure. foo.com (http-01): urn:acme:error:connection :: The server could not connect to the client to verify the domain :: Could not connect to foo.com

I double checked that port 80 is open, checked that the `.well-known` is accessible. Finally when diffing the certbot logs for both attempts I found that the production command actually tries `tls-sni-01` even though the error message says 'http-01`.

So this then finally worked:

`certbot certonly --webroot --webroot-path /var/www/html --preferred-challenges http-01 -d foo.com`
I think this may be a duplicate of #1819, which is is already fixed in Git master and will be shipped in 0.10.0.

The second traceback is later in execution than the first. I think the problem should ideally be caught at the same time as it was in the first traceback causing the same error type and message to be raised.

My instinct is we did all we reasonably could to warn them that the actions they were taking opted them out of security updates.

I had another idea too that left out of my last post for brevity and because I don't like it as much, but we could add a warning saying that the contents of the file will be overwritten unless you add `nginx-no-tls-updates = True` to add a `cli.ini` file in the same directory as the options. The problems I see with this approach are:

1. What about people who already modified the file?
2. Adding `--nginx-no-tls-updates` in any other way is extremely error prone. Running `certbot` just once, even if the Nginx plugin isn't used, may cause the file to be overwritten.
3. It's less conventional for sysadmins.
4. It's more aggressive and likely to break things.
5. For people that ignore and don't read our warnings, my instinct is to leave them alone and not force upgrades on them. This flips that behavior but may be what you want.

I personally like relying on distros and doing something special in `certbot-auto` better than that, but could maybe go down this route if problem 1 was reasonably addressed.

Now, you can either specify any combination of patterns to include (possibly none) or to exclude (possibly none). 

* No filters:

```
./watchman-make -t foo
# Relative to /Users/cotizo/watchman/python/bin
# Changes to any file will execute `make foo`
# waiting for change
```

* Filtering on included patterns:

```
./watchman-make -p **/.bar -t foo
# Relative to /Users/cotizo/watchman/python/bin
# Changes to files matching **/.bar will execute `make foo`
# waiting for changes
```

* Filtering on excluded patterns:

```
./watchman-make -x **/.baz -t foo
# Relative to /Users/cotizo/watchman/python/bin
# Changes to files not matching **/.baz will execute `make foo`
# waiting for changes
```

* Filtering on both included and excluded patterns:

```
./watchman-make -p **/.bar -x **/.baz -t foo
# Relative to /Users/cotizo/watchman/python/bin
# Changes to files matching **/.bar, but not matching **/.baz will execute `make foo`
# waiting for changes
```

Not specifying any `-p PATTERN` is not recommended, as any file (e.g. hidden files, short-lived files used by VCS or other processes) will trigger a make.

`watchman-make` is now limited to files only because, if the user uses only `-x PATTERN` without any `-p PATTERN`, then touching any file `//FOO/BAR` will also mark the folder `//FOO/` as dirty, which will in turn trigger a make.

> What is the advantage of moving some of the JS code from inline to a file? For instance the clock snippet/clock.js is basically unchanged.

A couple of advantages of doing that. 
- We'd be able combine our javascript and all the dependencies (like d3.js, nvd3.js) in one single minified chunk, which would be efficient and improve the page load time. 
- Write the test cases of our JS code by using separate files.

@yuanfeiz 
Background job coordinator and executor is definitely Airflow's use case. That said, we usually expect the heavy lifting to be done by the various systems Airflow talks to, not usually the airflow workers.

What this means is that usually, Airflow tasks are either : 
1) A sensor, which checks for a particular condition and returns True or False.
2) An operator which does delegates some work to a system (Say for instance a database, Spark or such)
3) A transfer operator, where we move data from one system to another, sometimes by using the local filesystem as an intermediate.

Airflow does not usually have tasks output data in the Python sense, mostly because there is no easy way in a distributed system to guarantee that the next task will be executed on the same worker, so loading the output of a task in memory is not usually useful.

For small amounts of data there the [XCom system](https://airflow.incubator.apache.org/concepts.html#xcoms) , which aims at being a lightweight cross communication system between tasks.

Maybe you could describe your use case a little better in a JIRA task [here](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AIRFLOW) or on the dev mailing list dev@airflow.incubator.apache.org, and we could see where to take this.

So, `devel_all` is a tuple of lists, which value is `(['sendgrid>=5.2.0', ..., 'azure-datalake-store==0.0.19'], ['atlasclient>=0.1.2'])`.

Thanks!

My view is that we should try to keep the build in the docker container, also on travis. I'll try to explain my thinking and why I think docker is the right tool.

1. It should be easy to reproduce the build environment locally. When a user builds (runs tests, analyses and so on) on his/hers local machine and the build passes, it should also pass on the build server. A common problem is that the versions of interpreters, frameworks and tools differs between development and build environment and lead to unpredictable results. A solution that keep them synchronized is preferable. 
1. Zero or little installation. There are tools in the python world (as you have shown) that handles versions and so on in a nice way, but we are also using other languages/tools/frameworks in other projects (ruby, php, C, java...). They all come with their own set of tools and solutions for versioning and handling environments. If we were only using one language, installing tools and setting up a environment might work out, but a common solution for all languages would be nicer. My hope is that by lowering the bar to get started, we can encourage more people to play with the code. At the same time I think it is important not to impose some specific tool chain on the user, that is if you want to install a full python environment on your machine that should work as well (and probably better than the general solution).
1. Decoupling from the build server. Travis is nice, but we might not use it forever and a generic solution will not tie us to travis.

My solution to this problem is using docker and the [toolbel](https://wiki.bitcraze.io/projects:dockerbuilderimage:index). It might or might not be the best solution? What are your thoughts?
